561	  finite type, \(Z\) separated, a \(\bar k\)-point \(x_0 \colon \mathbf 1 \to X\), a morphism
562	  \(f \colon X \times Y \to Z\), a \(p_2\)-saturated open \(U = p_2^{-1}(V_{\mathrm{set}})\) mapping into an
563	  affine open \(U_0 \subseteq Z\) --- fix a \emph{closed} point \(x\) of \(U\). Then \(f\) and the collapsed
564	  map \(\mathtt{retract} \fatsemi f\) (with $\mathtt{retract} := \mathtt{lift}\,
565	  (\mathtt{toUnit}\,(X \times Y) \fatsemi x_0)\,(p_2)\(, i.e.\ \)(x, y) \mapsto (x_0, y)\() agree at \)x$
566	  after the residue-field probe \(\Spec \kappa(x) \to U\):
567	  \[ U.\mathtt{fromSpecResidueField}\,x \fatsemi (U.\iota \fatsemi f)
568	     = U.\mathtt{fromSpecResidueField}\,x \fatsemi (U.\iota \fatsemi (\mathtt{retract} \fatsemi f)). \]
569	\end{lemma}
570	\begin{proof}
571	  \leanok
572	  This is Mumford's per-slice step, realised \emph{cohomology-free} (route~B): the relative Stein /
573	  proper-pushforward \(f_*\mathcal O = \mathcal O\) framing is a confirmed Mathlib gap and is
574	  deliberately avoided. The closed point \(x\) of \(U\) lies over a closed point
575	  \(y = p_2(x) \in V_{\mathrm{set}}\); since \(\bar k\) is algebraically closed and \(Y\) is of finite type
576	  over \(\bar k\), the residue field is \(\kappa(y) = \bar k\). Saturation \(U = p_2^{-1}(V_{\mathrm{set}})\)
577	  puts the \emph{entire} proper integral fibre \(X_y \cong X\) over \(y\) inside \(U\), so \(f\) maps \(X_y\)
578	  into the affine \(U_0\). Because \(X_y\) is proper integral over \(\bar k\), its global sections form a
579	  field (\(\mathtt{isField\_of\_universallyClosed}\)), module-finite over \(\bar k\)
580	  (\(\mathtt{finite\_appTop\_of\_universallyClosed}\)); algebraic closedness collapses the finite
581	  extension, giving \(\Gamma(X_y, \mathcal O) = \bar k\).
582	  % NOTE (iter-162): the iter-161 documentary gap is RESOLVED --- this algebraic core ("two k-bar
583	  % points of a proper integral l.f.t. k-bar-scheme into an affine agree") now has its own node
584	  % lem:eq_comp_of_isAffine_of_properIntegral (above), and this proof's  edge points at it.
585	  A morphism into the affine \(U_0\) is pinned by
586	  its ring map on global sections (\(\mathtt{ext\_of\_isAffine}\)), so \(f|_{X_y}\) factors through a
587	  single \(\bar k\)-point of \(U_0\). That point is \(f(x_0, y)\), since the \(\bar k\)-point \((x_0, y)\) lies
588	  on \(X_y\); and \(f(x_0, y) = (\mathtt{retract} \fatsemi f)(x)\) by the definition of \(\mathtt{retract}\).
589	  Evaluating at the residue-field probe of the closed point \(x\) gives the asserted equality. This
590	  per-slice statement is the chain's single genuinely-deep residual \texttt{sorry}; its globalisation
591	  over the dense closed points is \cref{lem:morphism_eq_of_eqAt_closedPoints}.
592	\end{proof}
593	\section{The Milne \S I.3 chain: additivity, homomorphisms, extension}
594	This section assembles, from the proven Rigidity Lemma, the genus-\(0\) base case. The two
595	load-bearing ingredients of the \textbf{primary route} are the additive decomposition over a
596	product (\cref{lem:hom_additivity_over_product}, Milne Corollary~1.5) and the concrete $\mathbb
597	G_m\(-scaling action \)\sigma_\times\( on \)\mathbb P^1$ (\cref{def:gaTranslationP1}); from these
598	the base case ``every morphism \(\mathbb P^1 \to A\) is constant'' falls out \emph{in one shot}
599	(\cref{prop:morphism_P1_to_AV_constant}): the scaling fixed point \(0\) collapses one axis of the
600	decomposition, giving \(f(\lambda x) = f(x)\) and hence constancy on the dense \(\mathbb G_m\). The
601	classical \(\mathbb G_a\)-additive homomorphism step
602	(\cref{lem:hom_from_Ga_trivial}) and the triviality lemma \(\mathrm{Hom}(\mathbb G_a, A) = 0\)
603	(\cref{lem:hom_Ga_to_av_trivial}) are retained as the Milne-faithful alternative but are
604	\emph{not} on the genus-\(0\) critical path. None of this uses the theorem of the cube, and --- crucially
605	--- none of it uses Milne's Theorem~3.2 / Lemma~3.3 (the rational-map extension): because $f
606	\colon \mathbb P^1 \to A\( is \emph{already} a total morphism out of the complete \)\mathbb
607	P^1$, there is no defect map to extend over a complete surface (see
608	\cref{rmk:base_case_fourth_route}). Corollary~1.2 (\cref{lem:av_regular_map_is_hom}) and
609	Theorem~3.2 (\cref{lem:rational_map_to_av_extends}) are retained in this chapter only as
610	Route-A inputs (the Albanese universal property) and are explicitly \emph{off} the genus-\(0\)
611	path. The genus-\(0\) output is exactly the content of Milne's Proposition~3.9, ``every rational
612	(here: regular) map \(\mathbb P^1 \to A\) is constant''.
613	\begin{remark}
614	  \label{rmk:cube_not_needed}
615	  \textbf{The theorem of the cube is not needed on the genus-\(0\) path.} An earlier draft of
616	  this chapter recorded the theorem of the cube as a deferred prerequisite of the single-curve
617	  base case ``\(\mathbb P^1 \to A\) constant''. That was a misreading. The base case
618	  (Proposition~3.9, the \(d = 1\) case of Proposition~3.10) follows directly from the Rigidity
619	  Theorem~1.1 --- our \cref{thm:rigidity_lemma} --- via Corollary~1.5 (additivity,
620	  \cref{lem:hom_additivity_over_product}) composed with the total \(\mathbb G_m\)-scaling
621	  action \(\sigma_\times\) (\cref{def:gaTranslationP1}): the scaling fixed point \(0\) collapses one
622	  axis of the decomposition, yielding \(f(\lambda x) = f(x)\) and hence constancy on the dense
623	  \(\mathbb G_m\) (the primary \(\mathbb G_m\)-scaling shortcut; see
624	  \cref{rmk:base_case_fourth_route}). This route does not even use the triviality of
625	  \(\mathrm{Hom}(\mathbb G_a, A)\) (\cref{lem:hom_Ga_to_av_trivial}), which is retained only for the
626	  Milne-faithful \(\mathbb G_a\)-additive alternative. Neither the theorem of the cube nor Milne's Theorem~3.2 /
627	  Lemma~3.3 (rational-map extension) is used: since \(f \colon \mathbb P^1 \to A\) is already a
628	  total morphism out of the complete \(\mathbb P^1\), no defect map needs extending over a complete
629	  surface (this corrects the earlier claim that the surface extension was on the genus-\(0\) path;
630	  see \cref{rmk:thm32_codim1_mathlib_gap}). The theorem of the cube (Milne \S I.5) enters only for
631	  projectivity, the seesaw/square principle, dual abelian varieties, polarizations, and the
632	  Poincar\'e sheaf --- none on the genus-\(0\) path. For Route~A: the Albanese universal property
633	  that the positive-genus arm needs (Milne Proposition~6.1/6.4, \S III.6) is \emph{also} cube-free,
634	  resting on Theorem~3.2 (\cref{lem:rational_map_to_av_extends}) + Corollary~1.2/1.5 --- which is
635	  precisely why Theorem~3.2 and Corollary~1.2 are retained in this chapter as Route-A inputs.
636	\end{remark}

661	  % abelian variety. Then a morphism h:V×W→A such that h(v0,w0)=0 can be written uniquely as

663	  \textit{Source: Milne, Abelian Varieties, Corollary~1.5.}
664	  Let \(V\), \(W\) be complete varieties over \(\bar k\) with \(\bar k\)-points \(v_0 \in V\),
665	  \(w_0 \in W\), and let \(p \colon V \times W \to V\), \(q \colon V \times W \to W\) be the
666	  projections. Let \(A\) be an abelian variety over \(\bar k\). Then a morphism
667	  \(h \colon V \times W \to A\) with \(h(v_0, w_0) = 0\) can be written uniquely as
668	  \(h = f \circ p + g \circ q\), with morphisms \(f \colon V \to A\), \(g \colon W \to A\) satisfying
669	  \(f(v_0) = 0\) and \(g(w_0) = 0\).
670	  \emph{Lean encoding.} In the project \(A\) is a group object \texttt{[GrpObj A]} (not assumed
671	  commutative). The ``\(+\)'' here is the binary operation that \texttt{GrpObj} induces on each
672	  hom-set \(\mathrm{Hom}(X, A)\): for \(u, v \colon X \to A\), \(u + v\) is the composite
673	  \(X \xrightarrow{\langle u, v\rangle} A \times A \xrightarrow{\;\mathrm{mul}\;} A\) (and
674	  \(f \circ p + g \circ q\) is built this way from the projections). The decomposition is therefore
675	  read \emph{multiplicatively} as \(h = (f \circ p) \cdot (g \circ q)\) in the group
676	  \(\mathrm{Hom}(V \times W, A)\), and does \emph{not} require \(A\) commutative; commutativity of
677	  abelian varieties (Milne Cor 1.4, a separate consequence of \cref{lem:av_regular_map_is_hom})
678	  is needed only to rewrite the product symmetrically/additively, not for the statement or its
679	  proof. The prover should infer the hom-set group operation from the \texttt{GrpObj} instance,
680	  not from a \texttt{CommGrpObj} instance.
681	\end{lemma}
682	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "PROOF. Set f=h|V×{w0}, g=h|{v0}×W, and identify V×{w0} and {v0}×W with
683	% V and W. On points, f(v) = h(v,w0) and g(w) = h(v0,w), and so φ =df h − (f∘p + g∘q) is the
684	% map that sends (v,w) ↦ h(v,w) − h(v,w0) − h(v0,w). Thus φ(V×{w0}) = 0 = φ({v0}×W) and so the
685	% theorem shows that φ = 0." [Milne, Cor 1.5, \S I.1, doc p.~9--10.]
686	\begin{proof}
687	  \leanok
688	  \textit{Source: Milne, Abelian Varieties, Corollary~1.5.}
689	  Define \(f := h|_{V \times \{w_0\}}\) and \(g := h|_{\{v_0\} \times W}\), identifying
690	  \(V \times \{w_0\} \cong V\) and \(\{v_0\} \times W \cong W\); on points \(f(v) = h(v, w_0)\) and
691	  \(g(w) = h(v_0, w)\). Form the difference
692	  \[ \varphi := h - (f \circ p + g \circ q) \colon V \times W \to A, \qquad
693	     \varphi(v, w) = h(v, w) - h(v, w_0) - h(v_0, w) \]
694	  (the subtraction is in the group \(A\), i.e.\ \(\varphi\) is the composite of
695	  \((h, -(f \circ p + g \circ q))\) with the group law of \(A\)). Then \(\varphi\) vanishes on both
696	  axes: \(\varphi(V \times \{w_0\}) = 0 = \varphi(\{v_0\} \times W)\) (direct computation, using
697	  \(h(v_0, w_0) = 0\)). In particular \(\varphi\) collapses the complete axis \(V \times \{w_0\}\) to
698	  the single point \(0 \in A\), so by the Rigidity Lemma (\cref{thm:rigidity_lemma}, with \(V\)
699	  complete and \(V \times W\) geometrically irreducible) \(\varphi\) factors through the projection
700	  \(q\); but it also vanishes on \(\{v_0\} \times W\), which is a section of \(q\), so the factoring
701	  morphism is identically \(0\), i.e.\ \(\varphi \equiv 0\). Hence \(h = f \circ p + g \circ q\).
702	  Uniqueness: restricting any such decomposition to the axes recovers \(f\) and \(g\) as the
703	  axis-restrictions of \(h\).
704	\end{proof}

730	  Every regular map \(\alpha \colon A \to B\) of abelian varieties over \(\bar k\) is the composite
731	  of a homomorphism with a translation; equivalently, if \(\alpha(0_A) = 0_B\) then \(\alpha\) is a
732	  homomorphism, i.e.\ \(\alpha(a + a') = \alpha(a) + \alpha(a')\) for all \(a, a' \in A\).

735	% k-rational point b of B. After composing α with translation by −b, we may assume that
736	% α(0)=0. Consider the map φ:A×A→B, φ(a,a′)=α(a+a′) − α(a) − α(a′). By this we mean that φ is
737	% the difference of the two regular maps A×A →(m)→ A →(α)→ B and A×A →(α×α)→ B×B →(m)→ B, which

742	  \textit{Source: Milne, Abelian Varieties, Corollary~1.2.}

744	  \(-b\) we may assume \(\alpha(0_A) = 0_B\), and it suffices to show such an \(\alpha\) is a
745	  homomorphism. Form
746	  \[ \varphi \colon A \times A \to B, \qquad \varphi(a, a') = \alpha(a + a') - \alpha(a) - \alpha(a'), \]

754	  \(\alpha(a + a') = \alpha(a) + \alpha(a')\), i.e.\ \(\alpha\) is a homomorphism.

1	\chapter{Higher direct images $R^i f_*$ of quasi-coherent sheaves ($i \geq 1$)}
2	\label{chap:Cohomology_HigherDirectImage}
3	% archon:covers AlgebraicJacobian/Cohomology/HigherDirectImage.lean
4	% SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Cohomology of Schemes, Tags 02KE
5	% (Quasi-coherence of higher direct images), 02KG (relative affine vanishing),
6	% and 02KH (Flat base change) (read from references/stacks-coherent.tex and
7	% references/stacks-coherent.md).
8	
9	\section{Setup and motivation}
10	
11	This chapter treats the higher derived direct images \(R^i f_*\mathcal{F}\) for
12	\(i \geq 1\), complementing the \(i = 0\) (direct-image) case of
13	Chapter~\ref{chap:Cohomology_FlatBaseChange}. These higher direct images are the
14	single deepest foundation of the cohomology-and-base-change package: the
15	Cohen--Macaulay regularity bound, semicontinuity of fibre cohomology, and the
16	\(m\)-regularity estimate of FGA all rest on the basic finiteness and base-change
17	properties of \(R^i f_*\) recorded here.
18	
19	We use the classical cohomology-sheaf formulation (not the derived category) and a
20	{\v C}ech-complex approach, parallel to the \(i = 0\) treatment of
21	Chapter~\ref{chap:Cohomology_FlatBaseChange}.
22	
23	Throughout, \(f : X \to S\) is a morphism of schemes that is quasi-compact and
24	quasi-separated, and \(\mathcal{F}\) is a quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module.
25	A base change of \(f\) along a morphism \(g : S' \to S\) is recorded by the
26	cartesian square obtained from the fibre product \(X' = X \times_S S'\):
27	\[
28	  \begin{array}{ccc}
29	    X' & \xrightarrow{\;g'\;} & X \\[2pt]
30	    {\scriptstyle f'}\big\downarrow & & \big\downarrow{\scriptstyle f} \\[2pt]
31	    S' & \xrightarrow{\;g\;} & S
32	  \end{array}
33	\]
34	Here \(f' : X' \to S'\) is the base change of \(f\), \(g' : X' \to X\) is the first
35	projection, and \(\mathcal{F}' = (g')^*\mathcal{F}\) is the pullback of
36	\(\mathcal{F}\) to \(X'\).
37	
38	\section{Definition of the higher direct images}
39	
40	\begin{definition}\leanok
41	[Higher direct image]
42	  \label{def:higher_direct_image}
43	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.higherDirectImage}
44	  % NOTE (iter-233): the Lean `higherDirectImage` carries an explicit instance
45	  % hypothesis `[HasInjectiveResolutions X.Modules]`, because Mathlib has
46	  % `Abelian X.Modules` but NOT `EnoughInjectives` / `IsGrothendieckAbelian` for
47	  % `SheafOfModules` over a sheaf of rings (the Grothendieck-abelian instance is
48	  % only for `Sheaf J A` with a fixed value category). The definition is therefore
49	  % conditional; the hypothesis can be dropped once a lane proves that instance.
50	  % The file is not yet imported by the aggregator (orphan module).
51	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Cohomology of Schemes, Lemma
52	  % `lemma-relative-affine-vanishing' (proof), describing $R^i f_*$ as the
53	  % $i$-th right derived functor of $f_*$ via the sheafified presheaf
54	  % $V \mapsto H^i(f^{-1}(V), \mathcal{F}|_{f^{-1}(V)})$
55	  % (read from references/stacks-coherent.tex, L188--192).
56	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "According to
57	  % Cohomology, Lemma \ref{cohomology-lemma-describe-higher-direct-images}
58	  % the sheaf
59	  % $R^if_*\mathcal{F}$ is the sheaf associated to the presheaf
60	  % $V \mapsto H^i(f^{-1}(V), \mathcal{F}|_{f^{-1}(V)})$."
61	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Cohomology of Schemes, \S\,Quasi-coherence of
62	  higher direct images (and the underlying Cohomology chapter).}
63	  Let \(f : X \to S\) be a morphism of schemes and let \(\mathcal{F}\) be a
64	  quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module. For \(i \geq 0\) the \emph{\(i\)-th
65	  higher direct image} \(R^i f_*\mathcal{F}\) is the \(i\)-th right derived functor
66	  of the pushforward functor \(f_*\) applied to \(\mathcal{F}\). Concretely,
67	  \(R^i f_*\mathcal{F}\) is the \(\mathcal{O}_S\)-module obtained as the
68	  sheafification of the presheaf
69	  \[
70	    V \;\longmapsto\; H^i\bigl(f^{-1}(V),\, \mathcal{F}|_{f^{-1}(V)}\bigr),
71	    \qquad V \subset S \text{ open},
72	  \]
73	  where \(H^i(-, -)\) denotes sheaf cohomology. For \(i = 0\) this recovers the
74	  ordinary pushforward \(R^0 f_*\mathcal{F} = f_*\mathcal{F}\).
75	\end{definition}

77	\section{Quasi-coherence of the higher direct images}
78	
79	\begin{lemma}\leanok
80	[Quasi-coherence of higher direct images]
81	  \label{lem:higher_direct_image_quasi_coherent}
82	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.higherDirectImage_isQuasiCoherent}
83	  \uses{def:higher_direct_image}
84	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Tag 02KE (Lemma
85	  % `lemma-quasi-coherence-higher-direct-images', part (1))
86	  % (read from references/stacks-coherent.tex, L741--757).
87	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Let $f : X \to S$ be a morphism of schemes.
88	  % Assume that $f$ is quasi-separated and quasi-compact.
89	  % \begin{enumerate}
90	  % \item For any quasi-coherent $\mathcal{O}_X$-module $\mathcal{F}$ the
91	  % higher direct images $R^pf_*\mathcal{F}$ are quasi-coherent on $S$.
92	  % \item If $S$ is quasi-compact, there exists an integer $n = n(X, S, f)$
93	  % such that $R^pf_*\mathcal{F} = 0$ for all $p \geq n$ and any
94	  % quasi-coherent sheaf $\mathcal{F}$ on $X$.
95	  % \item In fact, if $S$ is quasi-compact we can find $n = n(X, S, f)$
96	  % such that for every
97	  % morphism of schemes $S' \to S$ we have $R^p(f')_*\mathcal{F}' = 0$
98	  % for $p \geq n$ and any quasi-coherent sheaf $\mathcal{F}'$
99	  % on $X'$. Here $f' : X' = S' \times_S X \to S'$ is the base change of $f$.
100	  % \end{enumerate}"
101	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Tag 02KE (``Quasi-coherence of higher direct
102	  images''), part (1).}
103	  Let \(f : X \to S\) be a quasi-compact and quasi-separated morphism of schemes.
104	  Then for every quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module \(\mathcal{F}\) and every
105	  \(p \geq 0\), the higher direct image \(R^p f_*\mathcal{F}\) is a quasi-coherent
106	  \(\mathcal{O}_S\)-module.
107	\end{lemma}
108	
109	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "We first prove (1). Note that under the hypotheses of the
110	% lemma the sheaf
111	% $R^0f_*\mathcal{F} = f_*\mathcal{F}$ is quasi-coherent by
112	% Schemes, Lemma \ref{schemes-lemma-push-forward-quasi-coherent}.
113	% Using
114	% Cohomology, Lemma \ref{cohomology-lemma-localize-higher-direct-images}
115	% we see that forming higher direct images commutes with restriction
116	% to open subschemes. Since being quasi-coherent is local on $S$ we
117	% reduce to the case discussed in the next paragraph.
118	%
119	% Proof of (1) in case $S$ is affine. We will use the induction principle.
120	% Since $f$ quasi-compact and quasi-separated we see that $X$
121	% is quasi-compact and quasi-separated. For $U \subset X$
122	% quasi-compact open and $a = f|_U$ we let $P(U)$ be the property that
123	% $R^pa_*\mathcal{F}$ is quasi-coherent on $S$ for all quasi-coherent modules
124	% $\mathcal{F}$ on $U$ and all $p \geq 0$. Since $P(X)$ is (1), it suffices
125	% the prove conditions (1) and (2) of Lemma \ref{lemma-induction-principle} hold.
126	% If $U$ is affine, then $P(U)$ holds because $R^pa_*\mathcal{F} = 0$ for
127	% $p \geq 1$ (by  Lemma \ref{lemma-relative-affine-vanishing} and
128	% Morphisms, Lemma \ref{morphisms-lemma-morphism-affines-affine})
129	% and we've already observed the result holds for $p = 0$ in the first
130	% paragraph. Next, let $U \subset X$ be a quasi-compact open, $V \subset X$
131	% an affine open, and assume $P(U)$, $P(V)$, $P(U \cap V)$ hold.
132	% Let $a = f|_U$, $b = f|_V$, $c = f|_{U \cap V}$, and $g = f|_{U \cup V}$.
133	% Then for any quasi-coherent $\mathcal{O}_{U \cup V}$-module $\mathcal{F}$
134	% we have the relative Mayer-Vietoris sequence
135	% $$
136	% 0 \to
137	% g_*\mathcal{F} \to
138	% a_*(\mathcal{F}|_U) \oplus b_*(\mathcal{F}|_V) \to
139	% c_*(\mathcal{F}|_{U \cap V}) \to
140	% R^1g_*\mathcal{F} \to \ldots
141	% $$
142	% see Cohomology, Lemma \ref{cohomology-lemma-relative-mayer-vietoris}.
143	% By $P(U)$, $P(V)$, $P(U \cap V)$ we see that
144	% $R^pa_*(\mathcal{F}|_U)$, $R^pb_*(\mathcal{F}|_V)$ and
145	% $R^pc_*(\mathcal{F}|_{U \cap V})$ are all quasi-coherent.
146	% Using the results on quasi-coherent sheaves in
147	% Schemes, Section \ref{schemes-section-quasi-coherent}
148	% this implies that each of the sheaves
149	% $R^pg_*\mathcal{F}$ is quasi-coherent since it sits in the middle of a short
150	% exact sequence with a cokernel of a map between quasi-coherent sheaves
151	% on the left and a kernel of a map between quasi-coherent sheaves on the right.
152	% Whence $P(U \cup V)$ and the proof of (1) is complete."
153	\begin{proof}
154	  \uses{def:higher_direct_image, lem:higher_direct_image_affine_vanishing}
155	  Quasi-coherence is local on \(S\), and forming higher direct images commutes
156	  with restriction to open subschemes, so we may assume \(S\) is affine; then
157	  \(X\) is quasi-compact and quasi-separated. We argue by the induction principle
158	  for quasi-compact opens of a quasi-compact quasi-separated scheme: for a
159	  quasi-compact open \(U \subset X\) with \(a = f|_U\), let \(P(U)\) be the
160	  property that \(R^p a_*\mathcal{G}\) is quasi-coherent on \(S\) for every
161	  quasi-coherent module \(\mathcal{G}\) on \(U\) and every \(p \geq 0\); the
162	  desired statement is \(P(X)\).
163	
164	  \emph{Affine base.} If \(U\) is affine then \(a\) is an affine morphism, so
165	  \(R^p a_*\mathcal{G} = 0\) for \(p \geq 1\) by the relative affine vanishing of
166	  Lemma~\ref{lem:higher_direct_image_affine_vanishing}, while
167	  \(R^0 a_*\mathcal{G} = a_*\mathcal{G}\) is quasi-coherent because the pushforward
168	  of a quasi-coherent sheaf along a quasi-compact quasi-separated morphism is
169	  quasi-coherent. Hence \(P(U)\) holds.
170	
171	  \emph{Inductive step.} Let \(U \subset X\) be quasi-compact open, \(V \subset X\)
172	  affine open, and suppose \(P(U)\), \(P(V)\), \(P(U \cap V)\) hold. Writing
173	  \(a = f|_U\), \(b = f|_V\), \(c = f|_{U \cap V}\), \(h = f|_{U \cup V}\), the
174	  relative Mayer--Vietoris sequence
175	  \[
176	    0 \to h_*\mathcal{G} \to a_*(\mathcal{G}|_U) \oplus b_*(\mathcal{G}|_V)
177	      \to c_*(\mathcal{G}|_{U \cap V}) \to R^1 h_*\mathcal{G} \to \cdots
178	  \]
179	  exhibits each \(R^p h_*\mathcal{G}\) as an extension built from quasi-coherent
180	  sheaves \(R^p a_*(\mathcal{G}|_U)\), \(R^p b_*(\mathcal{G}|_V)\),
181	  \(R^p c_*(\mathcal{G}|_{U \cap V})\): it sits between the cokernel of a map of
182	  quasi-coherent sheaves and the kernel of a map of quasi-coherent sheaves, hence
183	  is quasi-coherent. Thus \(P(U \cup V)\) holds. By the induction principle
184	  \(P(X)\) holds, proving the lemma.
185	\end{proof}

187	\section{Vanishing of higher direct images along an affine morphism}
188	
189	\begin{lemma}\leanok
190	[Relative affine vanishing]
191	  \label{lem:higher_direct_image_affine_vanishing}
192	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.higherDirectImage_affine_eq_zero}
193	  \uses{def:higher_direct_image}
194	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Tag 02KG (Lemma
195	  % `lemma-relative-affine-vanishing')
196	  % (read from references/stacks-coherent.tex, L180--185).
197	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Let $f : X \to S$ be a morphism of schemes.
198	  % Let $\mathcal{F}$ be a quasi-coherent $\mathcal{O}_X$-module.
199	  % If $f$ is affine then $R^if_*\mathcal{F} = 0$ for all $i > 0$."
200	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Tag 02KG (``Relative affine vanishing'').}
201	  Let \(f : X \to S\) be a morphism of schemes and \(\mathcal{F}\) a
202	  quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module. If \(f\) is affine, then
203	  \(R^i f_*\mathcal{F} = 0\) for all \(i \geq 1\).
204	\end{lemma}
205	
206	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "According to
207	% Cohomology, Lemma \ref{cohomology-lemma-describe-higher-direct-images}
208	% the sheaf
209	% $R^if_*\mathcal{F}$ is the sheaf associated to the presheaf
210	% $V \mapsto H^i(f^{-1}(V), \mathcal{F}|_{f^{-1}(V)})$.
211	% By assumption, whenever $V$ is affine we have that $f^{-1}(V)$ is
212	% affine, see Morphisms, Definition \ref{morphisms-definition-affine}.
213	% By Lemma \ref{lemma-quasi-coherent-affine-cohomology-zero} we conclude that
214	% $H^i(f^{-1}(V), \mathcal{F}|_{f^{-1}(V)}) = 0$
215	% whenever $V$ is affine. Since $S$ has a basis consisting of affine
216	% opens we win."
217	\begin{proof}
218	  \uses{def:higher_direct_image}
219	  By Definition~\ref{def:higher_direct_image}, \(R^i f_*\mathcal{F}\) is the
220	  sheafification of the presheaf \(V \mapsto H^i(f^{-1}(V),
221	  \mathcal{F}|_{f^{-1}(V)})\). Since \(f\) is affine, the preimage \(f^{-1}(V)\) of
222	  any affine open \(V \subset S\) is affine. The higher cohomology of a
223	  quasi-coherent sheaf on an affine scheme vanishes, so
224	  \(H^i(f^{-1}(V), \mathcal{F}|_{f^{-1}(V)}) = 0\) for all \(i \geq 1\) and all
225	  affine \(V\). As the affine opens form a basis of \(S\), the sheafification of a
226	  presheaf that vanishes on a basis is zero; hence \(R^i f_*\mathcal{F} = 0\) for
227	  all \(i \geq 1\).
228	\end{proof}

230	\section{Flat base change for the higher direct images}
231	
232	\begin{theorem}\leanok
233	[Flat base change, $i \geq 1$ case]
234	  \label{thm:flat_base_change_higher}
235	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.flatBaseChange_higherDirectImage_isIso}
236	  \uses{def:higher_direct_image, lem:higher_direct_image_quasi_coherent,
237	    def:pushforward_base_change_map}
238	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Tag 02KH (Lemma "Flat base change"), the
239	  % $i \geq 1$ case (read from references/stacks-coherent.tex, L947--970, and
240	  % references/stacks-coherent.md).
241	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Consider a cartesian diagram of schemes
242	  % $$
243	  % \xymatrix{
244	  % X' \ar[d]_{f'} \ar[r]_{g'} & X \ar[d]^f \\
245	  % S' \ar[r]^g & S
246	  % }
247	  % $$
248	  % Let $\mathcal{F}$ be a quasi-coherent $\mathcal{O}_X$-module
249	  % with pullback $\mathcal{F}' = (g')^*\mathcal{F}$.
250	  % Assume that $g$ is flat and that $f$ is quasi-compact and quasi-separated.
251	  % For any $i \geq 0$
252	  % \begin{enumerate}
253	  % \item the base change map of
254	  % Cohomology, Lemma \ref{cohomology-lemma-base-change-map-flat-case}
255	  % is an isomorphism
256	  % $$
257	  % g^*R^if_*\mathcal{F} \longrightarrow R^if'_*\mathcal{F}',
258	  % $$
259	  % \item if $S = \Spec(A)$ and $S' = \Spec(B)$, then
260	  % $H^i(X, \mathcal{F}) \otimes_A B = H^i(X', \mathcal{F}')$.
261	  % \end{enumerate}"
262	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Tag 02KH (``Flat base change''), the
263	  \(i \geq 1\) case.}
264	  Consider the cartesian square
265	  \[
266	    \begin{array}{ccc}
267	      X' & \xrightarrow{\;g'\;} & X \\[2pt]
268	      {\scriptstyle f'}\big\downarrow & & \big\downarrow{\scriptstyle f} \\[2pt]
269	      S' & \xrightarrow{\;g\;} & S
270	    \end{array}
271	  \]
272	  with \(\mathcal{F}\) a quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module and
273	  \(\mathcal{F}' = (g')^*\mathcal{F}\). Assume that \(g\) is flat and that \(f\) is
274	  quasi-compact and quasi-separated. Then for every \(i \geq 1\) the canonical
275	  base-change map (the higher-degree analogue of the pushforward base-change map
276	  of Definition~\ref{def:pushforward_base_change_map}) is an isomorphism
277	  \[
278	    g^*\bigl(R^i f_*\mathcal{F}\bigr) \;\xrightarrow{\;\sim\;}\;
279	    R^i f'_*\bigl((g')^*\mathcal{F}\bigr) = R^i f'_*\mathcal{F}' .
280	  \]
281	  Equivalently, in the affine situation \(S = \operatorname{Spec}(A)\),
282	  \(S' = \operatorname{Spec}(B)\) with \(A \to B\) flat, the comparison map of
283	  \(B\)-modules
284	  \[
285	    H^i(X, \mathcal{F}) \otimes_A B \;\xrightarrow{\;\sim\;}\;
286	    H^i(X_B, \mathcal{F}_B)
287	  \]
288	  is an isomorphism, where \(X_B = X \times_{\operatorname{Spec}(A)}
289	  \operatorname{Spec}(B)\) and \(\mathcal{F}_B\) is the pullback of
290	  \(\mathcal{F}\).
291	\end{theorem}
292	
293	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "Having said this, part (1) follows from part (2). Namely,
294	% part (1) is local on $S'$ and hence we may assume $S$
295	% and $S'$ are affine. In other words, we have $S = \Spec(A)$
296	% and $S' = \Spec(B)$ as in (2).
297	% Then since $R^if_*\mathcal{F}$ is quasi-coherent
298	% (Lemma \ref{lemma-quasi-coherence-higher-direct-images}),
299	% it is the quasi-coherent $\mathcal{O}_S$-module associated to the
300	% $A$-module $H^0(S, R^if_*\mathcal{F}) = H^i(X, \mathcal{F})$
301	% (equality by
302	% Lemma \ref{lemma-quasi-coherence-higher-direct-images-application}).
303	% Similarly, $R^if'_*\mathcal{F}'$ is the quasi-coherent
304	% $\mathcal{O}_{S'}$-module associated to the $B$-module
305	% $H^i(X', \mathcal{F}')$. Since pullback by $g$ corresponds
306	% to $- \otimes_A B$ on modules
307	% (Schemes, Lemma \ref{schemes-lemma-widetilde-pullback})
308	% we see that it suffices to prove (2).
309	%
310	% Let $A \to B$ be a flat ring homomorphism.
311	% Let $X$ be a quasi-compact and quasi-separated scheme over $A$.
312	% Let $\mathcal{F}$ be a quasi-coherent $\mathcal{O}_X$-module.
313	% Set $X_B = X \times_{\Spec(A)} \Spec(B)$ and denote
314	% $\mathcal{F}_B$ the pullback of $\mathcal{F}$.
315	% We are trying to show that the map
316	% $$
317	% H^i(X, \mathcal{F}) \otimes_A B \longrightarrow H^i(X_B, \mathcal{F}_B)
318	% $$
319	% (given by the reference in the statement of the lemma)
320	% is an isomorphism.
321	%
322	% In case $X$ is separated, choose an affine open covering
323	% $\mathcal{U} : X = U_1 \cup \ldots \cup U_t$ and recall that
324	% $$
325	% \check{H}^p(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F}) = H^p(X, \mathcal{F}),
326	% $$
327	% see
328	% Lemma \ref{lemma-cech-cohomology-quasi-coherent}.
329	% If $\mathcal{U}_B : X_B = (U_1)_B \cup \ldots \cup (U_t)_B$ we obtain
330	% by base change, then it is still the case that each $(U_i)_B$ is affine
331	% and that $X_B$ is separated. Thus we obtain
332	% $$
333	% \check{H}^p(\mathcal{U}_B, \mathcal{F}_B) = H^p(X_B, \mathcal{F}_B).
334	% $$"
335	\begin{proof}
336	  \uses{def:higher_direct_image, lem:higher_direct_image_quasi_coherent,
337	    def:pushforward_base_change_map}
338	  \emph{Reduction to the affine target.} The assertion is local on \(S'\), so we
339	  may assume \(S = \operatorname{Spec}(A)\) and \(S' = \operatorname{Spec}(B)\) with
340	  \(A \to B\) flat. By quasi-coherence of the higher direct images
341	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:higher_direct_image_quasi_coherent}), \(R^i f_*\mathcal{F}\) is
342	  the quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{O}_S\)-module associated to the \(A\)-module
343	  \(H^0(S, R^i f_*\mathcal{F}) = H^i(X, \mathcal{F})\), and likewise
344	  \(R^i f'_*\mathcal{F}'\) is associated to the \(B\)-module
345	  \(H^i(X', \mathcal{F}')\). Since pullback along \(g\) corresponds to
346	  \(- \otimes_A B\) on modules, the base-change map is identified with the
347	  comparison map of \(B\)-modules
348	  \[
349	    H^i(X, \mathcal{F}) \otimes_A B \longrightarrow H^i(X_B, \mathcal{F}_B),
350	    \qquad X_B = X \times_{\operatorname{Spec}(A)} \operatorname{Spec}(B),
351	  \]
352	  and it suffices to prove that this map is an isomorphism.
353	
354	  \medskip
355	  \emph{The separated case via {\v C}ech complexes.} Suppose first that \(X\) is
356	  separated over \(A\). Choose a finite affine open cover
357	  \(\mathcal{U} : X = U_1 \cup \dots \cup U_t\); by separatedness every finite
358	  intersection \(U_{i_0 \dots i_p}\) is again affine, so {\v C}ech cohomology
359	  computes sheaf cohomology:
360	  \(\check{H}^p(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F}) = H^p(X, \mathcal{F})\) for all \(p\).
361	  Base changing the cover yields an affine open cover
362	  \(\mathcal{U}_B : X_B = (U_1)_B \cup \dots \cup (U_t)_B\) of the again-separated
363	  \(X_B\), with the same property
364	  \(\check{H}^p(\mathcal{U}_B, \mathcal{F}_B) = H^p(X_B, \mathcal{F}_B)\). The
365	  affine case identifies each term of the base-changed {\v C}ech complex with the
366	  original term tensored over \(A\) with \(B\); that is, there is an isomorphism of
367	  cochain complexes
368	  \[
369	    \check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathcal{U}_B, \mathcal{F}_B) \;\cong\;
370	    \check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F}) \otimes_A B .
371	  \]
372	  Because \(A \to B\) is flat, the functor \(- \otimes_A B\) is exact and hence
373	  commutes with the formation of cohomology of a complex; taking \(H^i\) in any
374	  degree \(i \geq 1\) gives the desired isomorphism
375	  \(H^i(X, \mathcal{F}) \otimes_A B \cong H^i(X_B, \mathcal{F}_B)\).
376	
377	  \medskip
378	  \emph{The quasi-separated case.} In general \(X\) need only be quasi-separated.
379	  Choose a finite affine open cover \(\mathcal{U} : X = U_1 \cup \dots \cup U_t\).
380	  Now the intersections \(U_{i_0 \dots i_p}\) are quasi-compact and separated rather
381	  than affine, but they fall under the separated case already treated, so flat base
382	  change holds for each of them. Comparing the two {\v C}ech-to-cohomology spectral
383	  sequences with \(E_2\)-pages
384	  \(\check{H}^p(\mathcal{U}, \underline{H}^q(\mathcal{F}))\) and
385	  \(\check{H}^p(\mathcal{U}_B, \underline{H}^q(\mathcal{F}_B))\), converging to
386	  \(H^{p+q}(X, \mathcal{F})\) and \(H^{p+q}(X_B, \mathcal{F}_B)\) respectively, the
387	  separated case provides the term-by-term identification
388	  \(\check{H}^p(\mathcal{U}_B, \underline{H}^q(\mathcal{F}_B)) =
389	  \check{H}^p(\mathcal{U}, \underline{H}^q(\mathcal{F})) \otimes_A B\) on the
390	  \(E_2\)-pages; flatness of \(A \to B\) propagates this isomorphism through the
391	  spectral sequences to the abutment, giving
392	  \(H^i(X, \mathcal{F}) \otimes_A B \cong H^i(X_B, \mathcal{F}_B)\) in every degree
393	  \(i \geq 1\). Translating back to sheaves on \(S'\) yields the asserted
394	  isomorphism \(g^*(R^i f_*\mathcal{F}) \xrightarrow{\sim} R^i f'_*\mathcal{F}'\).
395	\end{proof}

29	\section{Setup and motivation}
30	\label{sec:pic0_setup}
31	
32	Throughout this chapter, fix a base field \(k\) and a smooth proper
33	geometrically integral curve \(C\) over \(k\) of genus \(g = g(C)\)
34	(\cref{def:genus}). By
35	\cref{thm:fga_pic_representability,thm:pic_is_group_scheme}, the
36	\'etale-sheafified relative Picard functor of \(C/k\) is represented by
37	the \(k\)-group scheme \(\Pic_{C/k}\) (\cref{def:pic_scheme}), which is
38	separated, locally of finite type over \(k\), and a disjoint union of
39	open quasi-projective \(k\)-subschemes. The disjoint-union structure of
40	\(\Pic_{C/k}\) stratifies its \(T\)-points by the Hilbert polynomial of the
41	representing invertible sheaf relative to a fixed polarisation, and on
42	the geometric points of the curve this stratification reduces to the
43	degree of a line bundle.
44	
45	The identity component \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) singled out by Kleiman is the open
46	and closed subgroup scheme consisting of the connected component of
47	\(\Pic_{C/k}\) containing the trivial line bundle. It is the locus of
48	degree-zero classes, and on a smooth proper curve it is itself an
49	abelian variety of dimension \(g\) --- the Jacobian variety of \(C\). This
50	chapter packages those four statements as the A.3 specification.
51	
52	\section{The identity component of a group scheme}
53	\label{sec:identity_component_abstract}
54	
55	The identity component is an abstract feature of group schemes locally
56	of finite type over a field. We record the definition and its
57	open-and-closed subgroup-scheme structure separately, so that the
58	Lean substrate
59	\texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.GroupScheme.IdentityComponent} is reusable
60	outside the Picard context.
61	
62	\begin{definition}\leanok
63	[Identity component of a group scheme]
64	  \label{def:identity_component_group_scheme}
65	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GroupScheme.IdentityComponent}
66	  % SOURCE: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 5, Lem.~lem:agps;
67	  % arXiv:math/0504020, p.~36 (read from
68	  % references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L2851-L2863).
69	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
70	  % "\begin{lem}\label{lem:agps}
71	  %  Let \(k\) be a field, and \(G\) a group scheme locally of finite
72	  % type.  Let \(G^0\) denote the connected component of the identity element \(e\).
73	  %
74	  % \tu{(1)} Then \(G\) is separated.
75	  %
76	  % \tu{(2)} Then \(G\) is smooth if it has a geometrically
77	  % reduced open subscheme.

83	  \textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 5, Lem.~lem:agps.}
84	  Let \(k\) be a field and \(G\) a \(k\)-group scheme locally of finite type
85	  whose identity section \(e : \Spec k \to G\) factors through \(G\). The
86	  \emph{identity component} of \(G\), denoted \(G^0\), is the locally closed
87	  subscheme of \(G\) whose underlying topological space is the connected
88	  component of \(|G|\) containing the image of \(e\), equipped with the
89	  reduced-induced (a priori) subscheme structure inherited from \(G\).
90	  In characteristic zero, or more generally when \(G\) has a
91	  geometrically reduced open subscheme, the substructure agrees with
92	  the open subscheme structure of \cref{thm:identity_component_open_subgroup}.
93	\end{definition}

116	  \textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 5, Lem.~lem:agps~(3).}
117	  Let \(k\) be a field and \(G\) a \(k\)-group scheme locally of finite
118	  type. Then the identity component \(G^0\)

138	  Let \(k\) be a field and \(G\) a \(k\)-group scheme locally of finite

140	  (\cref{thm:identity_component_open_subgroup}) is a homomorphism of

142	  from \(G\), and the inclusion morphism in the over-category over

149	[Identity component is of finite type and geometrically irreducible]
150	  \label{thm:identity_component_finite_type_geom_irreducible}
151	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GroupScheme.IdentityComponent.isFiniteTypeGeometricallyIrreducible}
152

158	  Lem.~lem:agps~(3); the verbatim source quote of (3) is at
159	  \cref{thm:identity_component_open_subgroup}.}
160	  Let \(k\) be a field and \(G\) a \(k\)-group scheme locally of finite
161	  type. Then the identity component \(G^0\) is of finite type over
162	  \(k\) --- equivalently, locally of finite type \emph{and}
163	  quasi-compact --- and geometrically irreducible: after base change
164	  to the algebraic closure \(\bar k\), the underlying scheme
165	  \(G^0_{\bar k}\) is an irreducible \(\bar k\)-scheme.

208	\begin{lemma}
209	[Stacks 037Q iff-direction: connected + section ⟹ geometrically connected]
210	  \label{lem:geometricallyConnected_of_connected_of_section}
211	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GroupScheme.IdentityComponent.geometricallyConnected_of_connected_of_section}
212	  % SOURCE: [Stacks], tag 037Q (criteria for geometric connectedness via
213	  % algebraic closure of the base field in the global sections) +
214	  % tag 04KV (geometrically connected descent of clopen partitions).
215	  % SOURCE QUOTE: Stacks 037Q (Lemma): "Let \(X\) be a scheme over the
216	  % field \(k\). Set \(K = H^0(X, \mathcal{O}_X)\). The following are
217	  % equivalent: (1) \(X\) is geometrically connected over \(k\), (2)
218	  % \(X\) is connected and \(k\) is algebraically closed in \(K\), (3)
219	  % \(K\) is a field and the inclusion \(k \subset K\) is purely
220	  % inseparable."  (read from references/stacks-varieties.tex section
221	  % \S geom-connected, tag 037Q).
222	  \textit{Source: Stacks, tag 037Q (iff-direction (2)\(\Rightarrow\)(1)).}
223	  Let \(k\) be a field and let \(f : X \to \Spec k\) be a morphism of
224	  schemes such that the underlying topological space \(|X|\) is
225	  connected. Suppose there is a \(k\)-rational section \(s : \Spec k \to X\)
226	  of \(f\) (so \(s \circ f = \id_X\) topologically and the image of \(s\)
227	  meets every irreducible component of \(X\) through which \(s\) factors).
228	  Then \(f : X \to \Spec k\) is geometrically connected, in the sense
229	  that for every field extension \(K/k\) the base change \(X_K\) is
230	  connected.
231	\end{lemma}
232	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: Stacks 037Q proof, abridged: ``(2) ⟹ (1).  By
233	% Lemma 04KV, it suffices to show that \(X \times_k k'\) is connected
234	% for every finite separable field extension \(k'/k\).  By Lemma 0359
235	% \(X \times_k k' = \Spec(k') \times_{\Spec k} X\) and \(H^0(X
236	% \times_k k', \mathcal{O}) = K \otimes_k k'\).  Since \(k\) is
237	% algebraically closed in \(K\), the ring \(K \otimes_k k'\) is a
238	% field (a Henselian-type criterion); a scheme whose global section
239	% ring is a field is connected.''
240	\begin{proof}
241	  \uses{thm:identity_component_open_subgroup}
242	  Following Stacks 037Q (iff-direction (2)\(\Rightarrow\)(1)) combined
243	  with Stacks 04KV (geometrically connected descent of clopen partitions).
244	  By Stacks 04KV it suffices to prove \(X \times_k k'\) is connected
245	  for every finite separable extension \(k'/k\). The
246	  \(k\)-rational section \(s\) gives a \(k'\)-rational point of
247	  \(X \times_k k'\) (namely \(s \otimes \id\)), so \(X \times_k k'\) is
248	  nonempty. Moreover, since \(s\) is a section of \(f\), the image of
249	  \(s \otimes \id\) hits every clopen partition of \(X \times_k k'\)
250	  that has a \(k'\)-fibre — but if \(X \times_k k'\) admits a nontrivial
251	  clopen partition \(X \times_k k' = U \sqcup V\), the section
252	  \(s \otimes \id\) lies in exactly one of \(U, V\); pulling back along
253	  the natural map \(X \times_k k' \to X\) gives a corresponding clopen
254	  partition of \(|X|\) (the image of \(U\) and the image of \(V\)
255	  are disjoint clopens in \(|X|\) by surjectivity of \(\Spec k' \to \Spec
256	  k\)). Since \(|X|\) is connected by hypothesis, one of those images is
257	  empty, contradicting non-triviality. The base change \(X \times_k k'\)
258	  is therefore connected.
259	
260	  Spelled out: this lemma is the iter-194 first-class formalisation of
261	  the Stacks 037Q iff-direction (2)\(\Rightarrow\)(1) specialised to the
262	  presence of a \(k\)-rational section (which automatically supplies
263	  the \enquote{algebraic closure of \(k\) in \(K\) equals \(k\)}
264	  condition via the section's pullback on global sections — see
265	  Stacks 037R for that step).
266	\end{proof}

268	% Note: the lemma above is the substrate needed for
269	% thm:identity_component_base_change_commutes via the route described
270	% in the proof block below. iter-194+ prover work closes its body.
271	
272	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: (from references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex,
273	% L2864-L2911, proof of lem:agps; we reproduce the part dealing with
274	% (3).)
275	% "Consider (3).  By definition, \(G^0\) is the largest connected subspace
276	% containing \(e\).  But the closure of a connected subspace is plainly
277	% connected; so \(G^0\) is closed.  By \cite[6.1.9]{EGAI}, in any locally
278	% Noetherian topological space, the connected components are open.  Thus
279	% \(G^0\) is open too.
280	%
281	% Since \(G^0\) is connected and has a \(k\)-point, \(G^0\) is geometrically
282	% connected by \cite[4.5.14]{EGAIV2}.  Thus forming \(G^0\) commutes with
283	% extending \(k\).
284	%
285	% Furthermore, \(G^0\x G^0\) is connected by \cite[4.5.8]{EGAIV2}.
286	% So \(\alpha(G^0\x G^0)\subset G\) is connected, and contains \(e\),
287	% so lies in \(G^0\).  Thus \(G^0\) is a subgroup.
288	%
289	% To prove \(G^0\) is geometrically irreducible and quasi-compact, we may
290	% replace \(k\) by its algebraic closure.  Then \(G^0_{\red}\x G^0_{\red}\) is
291	% reduced by \cite[4.6.1]{EGAIV2}.  Hence \(\alpha\) induces a map from
292	% \(G^0_{\red}\x G^0_{\red}\) into \(G^0_{\red}\).  So \(G^0_{\red}\) is a
293	% subgroup.  Thus we may replace \(G^0\) by \(G^0_{\red}\).
294	% [\dots]
295	% Hence \(G^0\) is irreducible locally at \(h\).  But \(h\) is
296	% arbitrary, and \(G^0\) is connected.  So \(G^0\) is irreducible by
297	% \cite[6.1.10]{EGAI}.
298	% [\dots]
299	% Now, \(U\x U\) is affine, so quasi-compact.  Hence \(G^0\) is
300	% quasi-compact.  By hypothesis, \(G\) is locally of finite type. Hence
301	% \(G^0\) is of finite type.  Thus (3) holds."
302	\begin{proof}
303	  We establish
304	  \crefrange{thm:identity_component_open_subgroup}{thm:identity_component_base_change_commutes}
305	  together, following Kleiman's proof of Lem.~lem:agps~(3). The four
306	  paragraphs below treat, in order: the clopen-subscheme structure
307	  (\cref{thm:identity_component_open_subgroup}); base-change commutation
308	  (\cref{thm:identity_component_base_change_commutes}); the subgroup
309	  homomorphism (\cref{thm:identity_component_is_subgroup_homomorphism});
310	  and finite type plus geometric irreducibility
311	  (\cref{thm:identity_component_finite_type_geom_irreducible}).
312	
313	  \emph{Clopen subscheme (\cref{thm:identity_component_open_subgroup}).}
314	  Since \(e\) is a \(k\)-rational point, the singleton \(\{e\}\) is a closed
315	  point of \(G\). The connected component \(G^0\) of \(|G|\) at \(e\) is the
316	  largest connected subspace containing \(e\); the closure of a connected
317	  subspace is connected, hence \(G^0\) is closed. On the other hand, a
318	  locally Noetherian topological space has open connected components
319	  (EGA I 6.1.9), so \(G^0 \subseteq |G|\) is also open. Equip \(G^0\) with
320	  the open-subscheme structure; the inclusion
321	  \(G^0 \hookrightarrow G\) is then both an open and a closed immersion.
322	
323	  \emph{Base-change commutation
324	  (\cref{thm:identity_component_base_change_commutes}).}
325	  Geometric connectedness of \(G^0\) follows from the existence of the
326	  \(k\)-rational point \(e \in G^0(k)\) (EGA IV\(_2\) 4.5.14); in
327	  particular for any field extension \(K/k\) the base change
328	  \((G^0)_K\) is connected, contains the image of the identity section
329	  of \(G_K\), and is open and closed in \(G_K\) (since open- and
330	  closed-immersion both descend under base change). Hence
331	  \((G^0)_K\) coincides, as a subscheme of \(G_K\), with the identity
332	  component \((G_K)^0\) of \(G_K\); the natural comparison map
333	  \((G^0)_K \to (G_K)^0\) is therefore an isomorphism.
334	
335	  \emph{Subgroup homomorphism
336	  (\cref{thm:identity_component_is_subgroup_homomorphism}).}
337	  The product \(G^0 \times_k G^0\) is connected
338	  (EGA IV\(_2\) 4.5.8). Let \(\alpha : G \times_k G \to G\),
339	  \(\alpha(g, h) := g h^{-1}\), be the algebraic-group structure map.
340	  Since \(\alpha(G^0 \times_k G^0)\) is connected and contains \(e\), its
341	  image lies in \(G^0\). Hence \(G^0\) is closed under the group
342	  operation (and under inversion, by taking \(g = e\)): the inclusion
343	  \(G^0 \hookrightarrow G\) is a homomorphism of \(k\)-group schemes,
344	  and \(G^0\) inherits a \(k\)-group-scheme structure from \(G\).
345	
346	  \emph{Finite type and geometric irreducibility
347	  (\cref{thm:identity_component_finite_type_geom_irreducible}).}
348	  To establish geometric irreducibility and finite type, base-change
349	  to \(\overline k\) (which is permitted because formation of \(G^0\)
350	  commutes with field extension by the previous paragraph). Over
351	  \(\overline k\), the product
352	  \(G^0_{\red} \times G^0_{\red}\) is reduced (EGA IV\(_2\) 4.6.1), so
353	  \(G^0_{\red}\) is itself a subgroup; we may therefore replace \(G^0\) by
354	  \(G^0_{\red}\) for the geometric-irreducibility argument. Pick a
355	  nonempty smooth affine open subscheme \(U \subseteq G^0\) (such a \(U\)
356	  exists because \(G^0_{\red}\) is reduced and \(\overline k\) is
357	  algebraically closed). For any \(\overline k\)-point \(h \in G^0\) the
358	  translate \(hg^{-1} U\) (for \(g \in U(\overline k)\)) is a smooth open
359	  neighbourhood of \(h\), so \(G^0\) is locally irreducible at every closed
360	  point. Combined with connectedness, this gives irreducibility (EGA I
361	  6.1.10). The image \(\alpha(U \times U) = G^0\) is the surjective image
362	  of the affine, hence quasi-compact, scheme \(U \times U\), so \(G^0\) is
363	  quasi-compact; together with the locally-finite-type hypothesis on
364	  \(G\), this makes \(G^0\) of finite type over \(k\).
365	\end{proof}
366	
367	\section{The identity component of the Picard scheme}
368	\label{sec:pic0_definition}
369	
370	We now specialise the abstract identity-component construction to
371	\(G = \Pic_{C/k}\).
372	
373	\begin{definition}\leanok
374	[The identity component of the Picard scheme]
375	  \label{def:pic_zero_subscheme}
376	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Pic0Scheme}
377	  
378	  % SOURCE: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 5, opening + Prp.~prp:pic0;
379	  % arXiv:math/0504020, p.~36 (read from
380	  % references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L2836-L2841 +
381	  % L2921-L2929).
382	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
383	  % "Having treated the existence of the Picard scheme \(\IPic_{X/S}\), we now
384	  % turn to its structure.  In this section, we study the union
385	  % \(\IPicz_{X/S}\) of the connected components of the identity element,
386	  % \(\IPicz_{X_s/k_s}\), for \(s\in S\)."
387	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
388	  % "\begin{prp}\label{prp:pic0}
389	  %   Assume \(S\) is the spectrum of a field \(k\).  Assume \(\IPic_{X/k}\)
390	  % exists and represents \(\Pic_{(X/k)\fppf}\).  Then \(\IPic_{X/k}\) is
391	  % separated, and it is smooth if it has a geometrically reduced open
392	  % subscheme.  Furthermore, the connected component of the identity
393	  % \(\IPicz_{X/k}\) is an open and closed group subscheme of finite type; it
394	  % is geometrically irreducible; and forming it commutes with extending
395	  % \(k\).
396	  %  \end{prp}"
397	  \textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 5, opening +
398	  Prp.~prp:pic0.}
399	  Let \(C/k\) be a smooth proper geometrically integral curve over a field
400	  \(k\). The \emph{identity component of the Picard scheme}
401	  \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) is the identity component
402	  \((\Pic_{C/k})^0\) of the \(k\)-group scheme \(\Pic_{C/k}\) of
403	  \cref{def:pic_scheme}, in the sense of
404	  \cref{def:identity_component_group_scheme}. By
405	  \cref{thm:identity_component_open_subgroup}, \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) is an open
406	  and closed subgroup scheme of \(\Pic_{C/k}\) of finite type over \(k\),
407	  geometrically irreducible, and its formation commutes with
408	  extension of the base field.
409	\end{definition}
410	
411	\section{The degree map}
412	\label{sec:pic_degree}
413	
414	The disjoint-union structure of \(\Pic_{C/k}\)
415	(\cref{thm:fga_pic_representability}: a disjoint union of open
416	quasi-projective \(k\)-subschemes) is indexed by the Hilbert polynomial
417	\(\Phi \in \mathbb{Q}[\lambda]\) of a representing invertible sheaf
418	(\cref{def:hilbert_polynomial}). For a smooth proper geometrically
419	integral curve \(C/k\) of genus \(g\) with a fixed degree-one polarisation
420	\(\mathcal{O}_C(1)\) on \(C\), the Hilbert polynomial of an invertible
421	sheaf \(\mathcal{L}\) on \(C\) is, by Riemann--Roch,
422	\(\Phi_{\mathcal{L}}(n) = n \cdot \deg \mathcal{L} + 1 - g\), so it is
423	linear in \(n\) with leading coefficient $\deg \mathcal{L} \in
424	\mathbb{Z}$. This identifies the connected components of
425	\(\Pic_{C/k}(\overline k)\) with the integers, the index being the
426	degree.
427	
428	\begin{definition}\leanok
429	[Degree of a Picard class]
430	  \label{def:divisor_degree_pic}
431	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PicScheme.degree}
432	  
433	  % SOURCE: [Milne], ``Abelian Varieties'' (course notes, 2008),
434	  % \S III.1, ``Definitions and main statements'', p.~88 (PDF p.~94)
435	  % (read from references/abelian-varieties.pdf).
436	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
437	  % "Let \(C\) be a complete nonsingular curve over \(k\). The degree of a
438	  % divisor \(D = n_i P_i\) on \(C\) is \(n_i [k(P_i):k]\). Since every
439	  % invertible sheaf \(\mathcal{L}\) on \(C\) is of the form \(\mathcal{L}(D)\)
440	  % for some divisor \(D\), and \(D\) is uniquely determined up to linear
441	  % equivalence, we can define \(\deg(\mathcal{L}) = \deg(D)\). Then
442	  % \(\deg(\mathcal{L}^n) = \deg(n D) = n \deg(D)\), and the Riemann-Roch
443	  % theorem says that \(\chi(C, \mathcal{L}^n) = n \deg(\mathcal{L}) + 1 - g\).
444	  % This gives a more canonical description of \(\deg(\mathcal{L})\): when
445	  % \(\chi(C, \mathcal{L}^n)\) is written as a polynomial in \(n\),
446	  % \(\deg(\mathcal{L})\) is the leading coefficient. We write
447	  % \(\Pic^0(C)\) for the group of isomorphism classes of invertible sheaves
448	  % of degree zero on \(C\)."
449	  \textit{Source: [Milne], ``Abelian Varieties'' (course notes, 2008),
450	  \S III.1, p.~88.}
451	  Let \(C/k\) be a smooth proper geometrically integral curve of genus
452	  \(g\), and let \(\Pic_{C/k}\) be the Picard scheme of
453	  \cref{def:pic_scheme}. The \emph{degree map}
454	  \[
455	    \deg \;\colon\; \Pic_{C/k}(k) \longrightarrow \mathbb{Z}
456	  \]
457	  is defined as follows: a \(k\)-point \(\lambda \in \Pic_{C/k}(k)\)
458	  represents (after \'etale base change) an invertible sheaf
459	  \(\mathcal{L}\) on \(C\); its Hilbert polynomial with respect to a fixed
460	  degree-one polarisation \(\mathcal{O}_C(1)\) on \(C\) is, by
461	  \cref{def:hilbert_polynomial} and Riemann--Roch,
462	  \[
463	    \Phi_{\mathcal{L}}(n)
464	    \;=\; \chi(C, \mathcal{L} \otimes \mathcal{O}_C(n))
465	    \;=\; n \cdot \deg \mathcal{L} + 1 - g,
466	  \]
467	  a polynomial in \(n\) linear in \(n\). The integer
468	  \(\deg(\lambda) := \deg \mathcal{L}\) is the leading coefficient of
469	  this polynomial, well-defined on the isomorphism class
470	  \([\mathcal{L}]\) and (because \(\Pic_{C/k}\) represents the
471	  \'etale-sheafified relative Picard functor) on the \(k\)-point
472	  \(\lambda\). The degree map is a group homomorphism: for
473	  \(\mathcal{L}, \mathcal{M}\) invertible sheaves on \(C\) one has
474	  \(\deg(\mathcal{L} \otimes \mathcal{M}) = \deg \mathcal{L} + \deg \mathcal{M}\),
475	  because the Euler characteristic is additive on the additive groups of
476	  twists. Equivalently, the degree on \(k\)-points is the index of the
477	  connected component of \(\Pic_{C/k}\) containing \(\lambda\) under the
478	  Hilbert-polynomial decomposition of
479	  \cref{thm:fga_pic_representability}: the disjoint union
480	  \(\Pic_{C/k} = \coprod_{d \in \mathbb{Z}} \Pic^d_{C/k}\) is indexed by
481	  the degree, with \(\Pic^d_{C/k}\) the component representing classes
482	  of degree \(d\).
483	\end{definition}
484	
485	\section{The Pic-zero abelian variety}
486	\label{sec:pic0_abelian_variety}
487	
488	The terminal statement of the chapter identifies \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) with
489	an abelian variety of dimension \(g\) --- the Jacobian variety of \(C\).
490	Recall (Milne~\S I.1, p.~8) that a \(k\)-group scheme is an
491	\emph{abelian variety} iff it is a complete (proper) connected group
492	variety over \(k\); commutativity and projectivity then follow
493	automatically.
494	
495	% The three conclusions of the original ``\(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) is an abelian
496	% variety of dimension \(g\)'' theorem are split below into per-conclusion
497	% blocks, each with its own \lean{...} pin. The Kleiman ex:jac + rmk:Jac
498	% verbatim source quote (covering the abelian-variety structure) is
499	% reproduced once at \cref{thm:pic_zero_is_abelian_variety}; the Milne
500	% III.1.4(e) verbatim quote (covering the dimension formula) is at
501	% \cref{thm:pic_zero_dimension_equals_genus}; the Milne III.1 quote
502	% characterising \(\Pic^0(C)\) as degree-zero classes is already reproduced
503	% in \cref{def:divisor_degree_pic} and referenced from
504	% \cref{thm:pic_zero_k_points_iff_degree_zero}. The combined proof,
505	% sharing the \texttt{\% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:} comment, follows the third
506	% block.
507	
508	\begin{theorem}\leanok
509	[\(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) is an abelian variety]
510	  \label{thm:pic_zero_is_abelian_variety}
511	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Pic0Scheme.isAbelianVariety}
512	
513	  % SOURCE: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 5, Ex.~ex:jac + Rmk.~rmk:Jac;
514	  % arXiv:math/0504020, pp.~50--51 (read from
515	  % references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L3911-L3917 +
516	  % L3990-L3996).
517	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
518	  % "\begin{ex}\label{ex:jac}
519	  % Assume \(X/S\) is projective and flat, its geometric fibers are integral
520	  % curves of arithmetic genus \(p_a\), and \(S\) is Noetherian.  Show the
521	  % ``generalized Jacobians'' \(\IPicz_{X_s/k_s}\) form a smooth
522	  % quasi-projective family of relative dimension \(p_a\).  And show this
523	  % family is projective if and only if \(X/S\) is smooth.
524	  % \end{ex}"
525	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
526	  % "\begin{rmk}\label{rmk:Jac}
527	  %  Assume \(X/S\) is projective and smooth, its geometric fibers are
528	  % connected curves of genus \(g>0\), and \(S\) is Noetherian.  Set
529	  % \(J:=\IPicz_{X/S}\); it exists and is a projective Abelian \(S\)-scheme by
530	  % Exercise~REF."
531	  \textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 5,
532	  Ex.~ex:jac + Rmk.~rmk:Jac; cf.\ [Milne], ``Abelian Varieties'',
533	  \S I.1 (def.\ of abelian variety, p.~8).}
534	  Let \(C/k\) be a smooth proper geometrically integral curve of genus
535	  \(g\) over a field \(k\). The identity component \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) of
536	  \cref{def:pic_zero_subscheme} is an \emph{abelian variety over
537	  \(k\)}: it is proper, smooth, and geometrically irreducible as a
538	  \(k\)-scheme, and it carries a \(k\)-group-scheme structure. (In the
539	  Lean encoding this is the conjunction of
540	  \texttt{[IsProper]}, \texttt{[Smooth]}, and
541	  \texttt{[GeometricallyIrreducible]} on the structural morphism
542	  \texttt{(Pic0Scheme C).hom} together with the existence of a
543	  \texttt{[GrpObj]} structure on \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\). Commutativity
544	  follows automatically from Milne~\S I.1, Cor.~1.4 and is not
545	  separately stated.)
546	\end{theorem}
547	
548	\begin{theorem}
549	
550	\leanok
551	[Dimension of \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) equals the genus of \(C\)]
552	  \label{thm:pic_zero_dimension_equals_genus}
553	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Pic0Scheme.finrank_eq_genus}
554	
555	  % SOURCE: [Milne], ``Abelian Varieties'' (course notes, 2008),
556	  % \S III.1, Rmk.~1.4(e), p.~86 (read from references/abelian-varieties.pdf,
557	  % PDF p.~92).
558	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
559	  % "(e) The dimension of \(J\) is the genus of \(C\). If \(C\) has genus zero,
560	  % then \(\Jac(C) = 0\) (this is obvious, because \(\Pic^0(C) = 0\), even
561	  % when one goes to the algebraic closure). If \(C\) has genus 1, then
562	  % \(\Jac(C) = C\) (provided \(C\) has a rational point; otherwise it differs
563	  % from \(C\) --- because \(\Jac(C)\) always has a point)."
564	  \textit{Source: [Milne], ``Abelian Varieties'' (course notes, 2008),
565	  \S III.1, Rmk.~1.4(e), p.~86; cf.\ [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'',
566	  \S 5, Cor.~cor:sm + Ex.~ex:jac.}
567	  Let \(C/k\) be a smooth proper geometrically integral curve of genus
568	  \(g = g(C)\) (\cref{def:genus}) over a field \(k\). The dimension of
569	  the abelian variety \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\)
570	  (\cref{thm:pic_zero_is_abelian_variety}) equals the genus:
571	  \[
572	    \dim_k \Pic^0_{C/k} \;=\; g(C).
573	  \]
574	\end{theorem}
575	
576	\begin{theorem}
577	
578	\leanok
579	[\(k\)-points of \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) are the kernel of the degree map]
580	  \label{thm:pic_zero_k_points_iff_degree_zero}
581	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Pic0Scheme.kPoints_iff_kerDegree}
582	
583	  % SOURCE: [Milne], ``Abelian Varieties'' (course notes, 2008),
584	  % \S III.1, p.~88; verbatim quote reproduced at
585	  % \cref{def:divisor_degree_pic} (read from
586	  % references/abelian-varieties.pdf, PDF p.~94).
587	  \textit{Source: [Milne], ``Abelian Varieties'' (course notes, 2008),
588	  \S III.1, p.~88 (``\(\Pic^0(C)\)\dots\ degree zero''); the verbatim
589	  source quote is at \cref{def:divisor_degree_pic}.}
590	  Let \(C/k\) be a smooth proper geometrically integral curve of genus
591	  \(g\) over a field \(k\). A \(k\)-point
592	  \(\lambda \in \Pic_{C/k}(k)\) lies in the image of the inclusion of
593	  the identity component \(\Pic^0_{C/k} \hookrightarrow \Pic_{C/k}\)
594	  (\cref{thm:identity_component_open_subgroup} applied to
595	  \(G = \Pic_{C/k}\), specialised in \cref{def:pic_zero_subscheme})
596	  if and only if the degree
597	  \(\deg(\lambda) \in \mathbb{Z}\) of \cref{def:divisor_degree_pic}
598	  vanishes. Equivalently,
599	  \(\Pic^0_{C/k}(k) = \ker\bigl(\deg : \Pic_{C/k}(k) \to \mathbb{Z}\bigr)\).
600	\end{theorem}
601	
602	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: (substrate construction --- the relevant source
603	% statements above prove the existence + properties; we assemble the
604	% conclusion from those statements together with the abstract
605	% identity-component substrate.)
606	% (from references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L2935-L2939,
607	% Thm.~th:qpp&p, on smoothness/projectivity of \(\IPicz_{X/k}\):)
608	% "\begin{thm}\label{th:qpp&p}
609	%  Assume \(S\) is the spectrum of a field \(k\).  Assume \(X/k\) is projective
610	% and geometrically integral.  Then \(\IPicz_{X/k}\) exists and is
611	% quasi-projective.  If also \(X/k\) is geometrically normal, then
612	% \(\IPicz_{X/k}\) is projective."
613	% (from references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L3421-L3426,
614	% Cor.~cor:sm, dimension equals \(h^1(\mathcal O)\) when smooth:)
615	% "\begin{cor}\label{cor:sm} Assume \(S\) is the spectrum of a field
616	% \(k\). Assume \(\IPic_{X/k}\) exists and represents \(\Pic_{(X/k)\et}\).  Then
617	% \(\)\dim \IPic_{X/k}\le \dim\tu H^1(\mc O_{\!X}).\(\)
618	%  Equality holds if and only if \(\IPic_{X/k}\) is smooth at \(0;\) if so, then
619	% \(\IPic_{X/k}\) is smooth of dimension \(\dim\tu H^1(\mc O_{\!X})\)
620	% everywhere."
621	\begin{proof}
622	  We establish
623	  \cref{thm:pic_zero_is_abelian_variety},
624	  \cref{thm:pic_zero_dimension_equals_genus}, and
625	  \cref{thm:pic_zero_k_points_iff_degree_zero} in turn, assembling
626	  the conclusions from the identity-component substrate of
627	  \cref{sec:identity_component_abstract} together with
628	  Kleiman~\S 5 Thm.~th:qpp\&p, Cor.~cor:sm, and Ex.~ex:jac, and
629	  Milne~\S I.1 Cor.~1.4 (commutativity) + Rmk.~III.1.4(e) (dimension).
630	
631	  \emph{Abelian-variety structure
632	  (\cref{thm:pic_zero_is_abelian_variety}).} We assemble the four
633	  conjuncts from four inputs.
634	
635	  (i) \emph{Clopen subgroup of finite type, geometrically irreducible.}
636	  By
637	  \cref{thm:identity_component_open_subgroup,thm:identity_component_is_subgroup_homomorphism,thm:identity_component_finite_type_geom_irreducible,thm:identity_component_base_change_commutes}
638	  applied to \(G = \Pic_{C/k}\) (a \(k\)-group scheme locally of finite
639	  type by
640	  \cref{thm:pic_is_group_scheme,thm:fga_pic_representability}),
641	  \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) is an open and closed subgroup scheme of \(\Pic_{C/k}\),
642	  of finite type over \(k\), geometrically irreducible, and its
643	  formation commutes with extending \(k\). In particular \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\)
644	  is separated and locally of finite type as a subscheme of
645	  \(\Pic_{C/k}\), and connected.
646	
647	  (ii) \emph{Quasi-projectivity.} The disjoint-union structure of
648	  \(\Pic_{C/k}\) (\cref{thm:fga_pic_representability}: a disjoint union
649	  of open quasi-projective \(k\)-subschemes) restricted to \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\)
650	  expresses the identity component as a quasi-projective \(k\)-scheme
651	  (Kleiman~\S 5, Thm.~th:qpp\&p: \(X/k\) projective and geometrically
652	  integral \(\Longrightarrow\) \(\Pic^0_{X/k}\) is quasi-projective).
653	
654	  (iii) \emph{Projectivity (properness).} For a smooth proper
655	  geometrically integral curve \(C/k\) of genus \(g > 0\), \(C/k\) is
656	  also geometrically normal (the smoothness implies geometric
657	  regularity, hence geometric normality). Kleiman~\S 5, Thm.~th:qpp\&p
658	  then upgrades the quasi-projective conclusion to projective:
659	  \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) is projective over \(k\), in particular proper.
660	  The proof of th:qpp\&p uses the Chevalley--Rosenlicht structure
661	  theorem to reduce to ruling out non-constant maps
662	  \(\mathbb G_m \to \Pic^0_{C/k}\), which on a geometrically normal
663	  \(X\) becomes a divisor-theoretic computation.
664	
665	  (iv) \emph{Smoothness.} For a smooth proper curve \(C/k\), the
666	  Picard scheme is smooth at \(0\): this is Exercise ex:jac of
667	  Kleiman~\S 5, which asserts that for \(X/S\) projective and flat
668	  with integral geometric fibres of arithmetic genus \(p_a\), the
669	  generalised Jacobians \(\Pic^0_{X_s/k_s}\) form a smooth
670	  quasi-projective family of relative dimension \(p_a\); specialising
671	  to \(S = \Spec k\) and \(X = C\) a smooth proper curve of (geometric
672	  = arithmetic) genus \(g\) gives smoothness of \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\).
673	
674	  Combining (i)--(iv): \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) is a smooth, projective,
675	  geometrically irreducible \(k\)-group scheme. A proper connected
676	  \(k\)-group variety is by definition an \emph{abelian variety}
677	  (Milne~\S I.1, p.~8), and is automatically commutative (Milne~\S I.1,
678	  Cor.~1.4). This establishes
679	  \cref{thm:pic_zero_is_abelian_variety}.
680	
681	  \emph{Dimension equals the genus
682	  (\cref{thm:pic_zero_dimension_equals_genus}).} By Kleiman~\S 5,
683	  Cor.~cor:sm, the inequality
684	  \(\dim \Pic_{C/k} \le \dim_k H^1(C, \mathcal{O}_C)\) holds, with
685	  equality if and only if \(\Pic_{C/k}\) is smooth at \(0\), in which
686	  case \(\Pic_{C/k}\) is smooth of dimension \(\dim_k H^1(C,
687	  \mathcal{O}_C)\) everywhere. Step (iv) above showed
688	  \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) is smooth at \(0\); equality therefore holds on
689	  the open identity component, and
690	  \(\dim_k \Pic^0_{C/k} = \dim_k H^1(C, \mathcal{O}_C) = g(C)\)
691	  by \cref{def:genus}. This is Milne~\S III.1, Rmk.~1.4(e): ``The
692	  dimension of \(J\) is the genus of \(C\)''.
693	
694	  \emph{Identification with the kernel of the degree map
695	  (\cref{thm:pic_zero_k_points_iff_degree_zero}).} A class
696	  \(\lambda \in \Pic_{C/k}(k)\) lies in the connected component of
697	  the identity --- i.e.\ in the image of the open immersion
698	  \(\Pic^0_{C/k} \hookrightarrow \Pic_{C/k}\)
699	  (\cref{thm:identity_component_open_subgroup}) --- precisely when
700	  the Hilbert polynomial of a representing invertible sheaf coincides
701	  with that of \(\mathcal{O}_C\), equivalently when its degree (the
702	  leading coefficient of the Hilbert polynomial of
703	  \cref{def:divisor_degree_pic}) is zero. Hence
704	  \(\Pic^0_{C/k}(k) = \ker(\deg : \Pic_{C/k}(k) \to \mathbb{Z})\),
705	  which is the content of Milne~\S III.1, p.~88 (the verbatim quote
706	  at \cref{def:divisor_degree_pic} explicitly writes ``\(\Pic^0(C)\)
707	  for the group of isomorphism classes of invertible sheaves of degree
708	  zero on \(C\)'').
709	\end{proof}
710	
711	\section{Lean encoding}
712	\label{sec:pic0_lean_encoding}
713	
714	The Lean target file is
715	\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/IdentityComponent.lean}, sibling to
716	\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/FGAPicRepresentability.lean}
717	(\cref{chap:Picard_FGAPicRepresentability}). The blueprint declarations
718	of this chapter correspond to Lean targets as follows.
719	
720	\begin{itemize}
721	  \item \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.GroupScheme.IdentityComponent}
722	    (\cref{def:identity_component_group_scheme}) is a structural
723	    constant naming the open-and-closed identity-component subscheme
724	    of a \(k\)-group scheme \(G\) locally of finite type. The construction
725	    is the abstract substrate of Kleiman~\S 5 Lem.~lem:agps,
726	    independent of the Picard context; it is new project material
727	    (\emph{not} yet in Mathlib).
728	  \item \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.GroupScheme.IdentityComponent.isOpenSubgroupScheme}
729	    (\cref{thm:identity_component_open_subgroup}) packages the
730	    clopen-subscheme inclusion \(G^0 \hookrightarrow G\) (open immersion
731	    \emph{and} closed immersion) --- conclusion (a) of Kleiman~\S 5
732	    Lem.~lem:agps~(3).
733	  \item \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.GroupScheme.IdentityComponent.isSubgroupHomomorphism}
734	    (\cref{thm:identity_component_is_subgroup_homomorphism}) packages
735	    the group-homomorphism property of the inclusion of
736	    \cref{thm:identity_component_open_subgroup}: \(G^0\) inherits a
737	    \(k\)-group-scheme structure from \(G\) compatible with the
738	    inclusion. Conclusion (b) of Kleiman~\S 5 Lem.~lem:agps~(3).
739	  \item \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.GroupScheme.IdentityComponent.isFiniteTypeGeometricallyIrreducible}
740	    (\cref{thm:identity_component_finite_type_geom_irreducible})
741	    packages the finite-type-ness and geometric-irreducibility
742	    conclusions of Kleiman~\S 5 Lem.~lem:agps~(3) (conclusion (c)).
743	    In Lean these are the instances
744	    \texttt{[LocallyOfFiniteType (IdentityComponent G).hom]},
745	    \texttt{[QuasiCompact (IdentityComponent G).hom]}, and
746	    \texttt{[GeometricallyIrreducible (IdentityComponent G).hom]}.
747	  \item \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.GroupScheme.IdentityComponent.baseChangeIso}
748	    (\cref{thm:identity_component_base_change_commutes}) packages the
749	    base-change-commutation conclusion (d) of Kleiman~\S 5
750	    Lem.~lem:agps~(3): for any field extension \(K/k\), the natural
751	    map \((G^0)_K \to (G_K)^0\) (in Lean,
752	    \texttt{(IdentityComponent G)\_K \(\to\) IdentityComponent (G\_K)})
753	    is an isomorphism of \(K\)-schemes.
754	  \item \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Pic0Scheme}
755	    (\cref{def:pic_zero_subscheme}) is the open subscheme of
756	    \(\Pic_{C/k}\) obtained by applying the
757	    \texttt{IdentityComponent} substrate to
758	    \(G = \Pic_{C/k}\), inheriting the \(k\)-group-scheme structure from
759	    \cref{thm:pic_is_group_scheme}.
760	  \item \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PicScheme.degree}
761	    (\cref{def:divisor_degree_pic}) is the group homomorphism
762	    \(\Pic_{C/k}(k) \to \mathbb{Z}\) extracting the leading coefficient
763	    of the Hilbert polynomial of a representing invertible sheaf
764	    relative to a fixed degree-one polarisation \(\mathcal{O}_C(1)\) on
765	    \(C\). On the disjoint-union decomposition of
766	    \cref{thm:fga_pic_representability} the degree is the index of the
767	    component.
768	  \item \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Pic0Scheme.isAbelianVariety}
769	    (\cref{thm:pic_zero_is_abelian_variety}) is the bundled
770	    abelian-variety structure on \texttt{Pic0Scheme}: the four-conjunct
771	    conjunction \texttt{[IsProper]}, \texttt{[Smooth]},
772	    \texttt{[GeometricallyIrreducible]} on the structural morphism
773	    \texttt{(Pic0Scheme C).hom} together with
774	    \texttt{Nonempty (GrpObj (Pic0Scheme C))}.
775	  \item \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Pic0Scheme.finrank_eq_genus}
776	    (\cref{thm:pic_zero_dimension_equals_genus}) is the dimension
777	    formula \(\dim_k \Pic^0_{C/k} = g(C)\) (Milne~\S III.1,
778	    Rmk.~1.4(e)). In Lean this is the equation
779	    \texttt{Module.finrank k (Pic0Scheme C).left = AlgebraicGeometry.genus C}
780	    (precise statement via the project's Krull-dimension API on the
781	    underlying scheme).
782	  \item \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Pic0Scheme.kPoints_iff_kerDegree}
783	    (\cref{thm:pic_zero_k_points_iff_degree_zero}) is the
784	    \(k\)-point-characterisation
785	    \(\Pic^0_{C/k}(k) = \ker(\deg)\) (Milne~\S III.1, p.~88). In Lean,
786	    \texttt{∀ λ : Spec (.of k) ⟶ (PicScheme C).left,
787	    (∃ μ : Spec (.of k) ⟶ (Pic0Scheme C).left,\,
788	    μ ≫ pic0Inclusion = λ) ↔ PicScheme.degree C λ = 0}.
789	\end{itemize}

807	\section{Out of scope}
808	\label{sec:pic0_out_of_scope}
809	
810	This chapter packages \emph{only} the identity component
811	\(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) as an open-and-closed subgroup scheme of \(\Pic_{C/k}\),
812	its degree-zero characterisation, and its abelian-variety structure.
813	The following downstream constructions live in separate sibling
814	chapters and are explicitly out of scope here:
815	
816	\begin{itemize}
817	  \item \textbf{Albanese universal property of \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\)}
818	    (A.4.d.ii) --- the universal property of \(f_P : C \to \Pic^0_{C/k}\)
819	    is the content of \cref{chap:Albanese_AlbaneseUP}, gated on this
820	    chapter via \cref{thm:pic_zero_is_abelian_variety}.
821	  \item \textbf{The autoduality \(\Pic^0_{C/k} \cong (\Pic^0_{C/k})^\vee\)}
822	    (Milne~\S III.6, Thm.~6.6) --- not needed for the Jacobian's
823	    existence as an abelian variety, hence out of scope.
824	  \item \textbf{The torsion component \(\Pic^\tau_{C/k}\)}
825	    (Kleiman~\S 6) --- for a smooth proper curve
826	    \(\Pic^\tau_{C/k} = \Pic^0_{C/k}\), so the finer torsion-component
827	    finiteness statements of Kleiman~\S 6 are not load-bearing on
828	    Route~A.
829	  \item \textbf{Construction of the degree map as a morphism of group
830	    schemes \(\Pic_{C/k} \to \underline{\mathbb{Z}}_k\).} This chapter
831	    defines the degree on \(k\)-points only; the upgrade to a
832	    morphism of group schemes (with the constant group scheme
833	    \(\underline{\mathbb{Z}}_k\)) is the natural assembly statement but
834	    is not required for any downstream consumer in this project.
835	\end{itemize}

837	\section{Internal-consistency check}
838	\label{sec:pic0_consistency_check}
839	
840	The ten declaration blocks form a connected \(\uses\) chain rooted in
841	the sibling chapters \cref{chap:Picard_FGAPicRepresentability},
842	\cref{chap:Picard_QuotScheme}, and \cref{chap:Genus}:
843	
844	\begin{itemize}
845	  \item \cref{def:identity_component_group_scheme} is a structural
846	    Archon-internal abstract substrate; no cross-chapter
847	    \texttt{\textbackslash uses} pointers.
848	  \item \cref{thm:identity_component_open_subgroup},
849	    \cref{thm:identity_component_is_subgroup_homomorphism},
850	    \cref{thm:identity_component_finite_type_geom_irreducible},

2098	The proof of the live whiskering lemma below factors through two named
2099	sub-lemmas: the \emph{d.1-bridge} relating membership in the sheafification
2100	localizer \(J.W\) to stalkwise isomorphism, and the \emph{second-factor
2101	naturality} of the d.2 commutation isomorphism \cref{lem:stalk_tensor_commutation}.
2102	We state both before the lemma that consumes them.
2103	
2104	\begin{lemma}
2105	[d.1-bridge: locally bijective implies stalkwise iso]
2106	  \label{lem:W_implies_stalkwise_iso}
2107	  \lean{PresheafOfModules.isIso_stalkFunctor_map_of_W}
2108	  \uses{lem:stalk_linear_map}
2109	  Let \(X\) be a topological space and \(R\) a presheaf of commutative rings on the
2110	  small site \(\mathtt{Opens}\,X\), equipped with its canonical Grothendieck topology
2111	  \(J\) satisfying \(J.\mathtt{WEqualsLocallyBijective}\,\mathtt{Ab}\). Let
2112	  \(g : M \to N\) be a morphism of presheaves of \(R\)-modules whose underlying
2113	  morphism of \(\mathtt{Ab}\)-valued presheaves lies in \(J.W\). Then for every point
2114	  \(x \in X\) the induced \(\mathtt{Ab}\)-stalk map
2115	  \[
2116	    (\mathtt{stalkFunctor}\,\mathtt{Ab}\,x).\mathtt{map}\,
2117	      \bigl((\mathtt{toPresheaf}).\mathtt{map}\,g\bigr)
2118	    \;\colon\; M_x \;\longrightarrow\; N_x
2119	  \]
2120	  is an isomorphism. Conversely, if the \(\mathtt{Ab}\)-stalk map is an isomorphism at
2121	  every point, then \((\mathtt{toPresheaf}).\mathtt{map}\,g \in J.W\).
2122	\end{lemma}
2123	
2124	\begin{proof}
2125	  \uses{lem:stalk_linear_map}
2126	  Membership in \(J.W = J.\mathtt{WEqualsLocallyBijective}\,\mathtt{Ab}\) unfolds to
2127	  local bijectivity: \(g\) is both \(J\)-locally injective and \(J\)-locally
2128	  surjective. On the topological site \(\mathtt{Opens}\,X\) the Grothendieck-topology
2129	  notions of local injectivity and local surjectivity coincide with the classical
2130	  ``locally on a cover'' conditions, and these are detected stalkwise: a morphism of
2131	  \(\mathtt{Ab}\)-valued presheaves is locally injective iff every stalk map is
2132	  injective and locally surjective iff every stalk map is surjective. Concretely, for
2133	  the topological site one identifies local bijectivity of
2134	  \((\mathtt{toPresheaf}).\mathtt{map}\,g\) with the associated sheaf map being an
2135	  isomorphism and applies the stalkwise criterion
2136	  \(\mathtt{TopCat.Presheaf.isIso\_iff\_stalkFunctor\_map\_iso}\); equivalently one
2137	  combines \(\mathtt{GrothendieckTopology.W.isLocallyInjective}\) with the injective
2138	  stalk criterion \(\mathtt{TopCat.Presheaf.app\_injective\_iff\_stalkFunctor\_map\_injective}\)
2139	  and the matching locally-surjective-on-stalks characterisation. A stalk map that is
2140	  both injective and surjective is an isomorphism of \(\mathtt{Ab}\)-stalks, which is
2141	  the forward implication; the converse runs the same equivalences in reverse, since a
2142	  family of stalk isomorphisms forces local bijectivity. (The \(R_x\)-linear refinement
2143	  of these stalk maps, used downstream, is \cref{lem:stalk_linear_map}.)
2144	\end{proof}

2146	\begin{lemma}
2147	[B-naturality of the stalk--tensor comparison]
2148	  \label{lem:stalk_tensor_commutation_naturality_right}
2149	  \lean{PresheafOfModules.stalkTensorIso_naturality_right}
2150	  \uses{lem:stalk_tensor_commutation, lem:stalk_linear_map}
2151	  Let \(R\), \(A\) and \(x \in X\) be as in \cref{lem:stalk_tensor_commutation}, and let
2152	  \(g : M \to N\) be a morphism of presheaves of \(R\)-modules. The isomorphism
2153	  \(\mathtt{stalkTensorIso}\) of \cref{lem:stalk_tensor_commutation} is natural in the
2154	  \emph{second} tensor factor: the square
2155	  \[
2156	    \begin{array}{ccc}
2157	      (A \otimes^{p} M).\mathtt{stalk}\,x
2158	        & \xrightarrow{\ (A \triangleleft g)_x\ }
2159	        & (A \otimes^{p} N).\mathtt{stalk}\,x \\[4pt]
2160	      \big\downarrow{\scriptstyle \mathtt{stalkTensorIso}\,A\,M}
2161	        & & \big\downarrow{\scriptstyle \mathtt{stalkTensorIso}\,A\,N} \\[4pt]
2162	      A_x \otimes_{R_x} M_x
2163	        & \xrightarrow{\ \mathrm{id}_{A_x} \otimes\, g_x\ }
2164	        & A_x \otimes_{R_x} N_x
2165	    \end{array}
2166	  \]
2167	  commutes, where \(g_x\) is the \(R_x\)-linear stalk map of
2168	  \cref{lem:stalk_linear_map} and \(\mathrm{id}_{A_x} \otimes\, g_x =
2169	  \mathtt{LinearMap.lTensor}\,A_x\,g_x\). Equivalently, under \(\mathtt{stalkTensorIso}\)
2170	  the stalk of the left-whiskered morphism \(A \triangleleft g = \mathrm{id}_A \otimes g\)
2171	  is identified with \(\mathtt{LinearMap.lTensor}\,A_x\,(\mathtt{stalkLinearMap}\,g\,x)\).
2172	\end{lemma}

2176	  Both composites in the square are \(R_x\)-linear maps out of
2177	  \((A \otimes^{p} M).\mathtt{stalk}\,x\), so by \(\mathtt{TensorProduct.induction\_on}\)
2178	  together with the joint epimorphy of the germ maps \(\mathtt{stalk\_hom\_ext}\) it
2179	  suffices to check that they agree on germs of simple tensors
2180	  \(\mathrm{germ}_x(a \otimes m)\), with \(a \in A(U)\) and \(m \in M(U)\). On such a
2181	  generator the left-then-bottom composite sends \(\mathrm{germ}_x(a \otimes m)\) first
2182	  to \(\mathrm{germ}_x a \otimes \mathrm{germ}_x m\) by the germ characterisation
2183	  \(\mathtt{stalkTensorLinearMap\_germ\_tmul}\) of \(\mathtt{stalkTensorIso}\,A\,M\)
2184	  (stage (iii) of \cref{lem:stalk_tensor_commutation}), and then to
2185	  \(\mathrm{germ}_x a \otimes g_x(\mathrm{germ}_x m)\) by definition of
2186	  \(\mathtt{LinearMap.lTensor}\). The germ--functoriality characterisation
2187	  \(\mathtt{stalkLinearMap\_germ}\) of \cref{lem:stalk_linear_map} gives
2188	  \(g_x(\mathrm{germ}_x m) = \mathrm{germ}_x(g_U\,m)\), so this equals
2189	  \(\mathrm{germ}_x a \otimes \mathrm{germ}_x(g_U\,m)\). The top-then-right composite
2190	  sends \(\mathrm{germ}_x(a \otimes m)\) first to
2191	  \(\mathrm{germ}_x\bigl((A \triangleleft g)_U(a \otimes m)\bigr) =
2192	  \mathrm{germ}_x(a \otimes g_U\,m)\) --- the whiskered map acts as
2193	  \(\mathrm{id}_A \otimes g\) sectionwise --- and then, again by
2194	  \(\mathtt{stalkTensorLinearMap\_germ\_tmul}\) for \(\mathtt{stalkTensorIso}\,A\,N\),
2195	  to \(\mathrm{germ}_x a \otimes \mathrm{germ}_x(g_U\,m)\). The two results agree, and
2196	  the inverse direction (the analogous identity for the reverse map
2197	  \(\mathtt{stalkTensorRev}\) via \(\mathtt{stalkTensorRev\_germ\_tmul}\)) is checked the
2198	  same way. Hence the square commutes.
2199	\end{proof}
