2201	\begin{lemma}
2202	
2203	\leanok
2204	[Unconditional left-whiskering of the localizer, via d.2]
2205	  \label{lem:islocallyinjective_whiskerleft_via_stalk}
2206	  \lean{PresheafOfModules.isLocallyInjective_whiskerLeft_of_W}
2207	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj, lem:stalk_tensor_commutation, lem:stalk_linear_map}
2208	  Let \(R\), \(J\) be as above with \(J.\mathtt{WEqualsLocallyBijective}\,\mathtt{Ab}\),
2209	  let \(F\) be an \emph{arbitrary} presheaf of \(R\)-modules, and let \(g : M \to N\)
2210	  be a morphism whose underlying morphism lies in \(J.W\). Then the left-whiskered
2211	  morphism \(F \triangleleft g = \mathrm{id}_F \otimes g\) is \(J\)-locally injective
2212	  (indeed lies in \(J.W\)). No flatness and no local-triviality hypothesis on \(F\) is
2213	  required. This discharges the open left-whiskering obligation
2214	  \cref{lem:islocallyinjective_whisker_of_W} that the unconditional associator
2215	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso} consumes.
2216	\end{lemma}
2217	
2218	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: (Archon-original assembly from the Stacks stalk-tensor lemma
2219	% above and the stalkwise criterion for membership in the sheafification localizer;
2220	% no single external source proof.)
2221	\begin{proof}
2222	  \uses{lem:stalk_tensor_commutation, lem:stalk_linear_map,
2223	    lem:W_implies_stalkwise_iso, lem:stalk_tensor_commutation_naturality_right}
2224	  The proof runs in three movements: the d.1-bridge turns membership in \(J.W\) into a
2225	  stalkwise statement, the second-factor naturality of the d.2 isomorphism transports
2226	  the whiskered stalk into a tensor of the stalk maps, and a flatness-free
2227	  ``tensoring by an equivalence'' argument concludes.
2228	
2229	  \emph{(a) The d.1-bridge.} Specialise to the topological site \(\mathtt{Opens}\,X\),
2230	  where stalks exist. By the hypothesis \(g \in J.W\) and the d.1-bridge
2231	  \cref{lem:W_implies_stalkwise_iso}, the \(\mathtt{Ab}\)-stalk map \(g_x : M_x \to N_x\)
2232	  is an isomorphism at every point \(x \in X\). By \cref{lem:stalk_linear_map} this
2233	  stalk map carries an \(R_x\)-linear structure, and the bijectivity just obtained
2234	  upgrades it to an \(R_x\)-linear equivalence
2235	  \(\mathtt{stalkLinearEquivOfIsIso} : M_x \xrightarrow{\sim} N_x\).
2236	
2237	  \emph{(b) Identifying the whiskered stalk.} By the second-factor naturality of the
2238	  stalk--tensor comparison \cref{lem:stalk_tensor_commutation_naturality_right}, the
2239	  isomorphism \(\mathtt{stalkTensorIso}\) of \cref{lem:stalk_tensor_commutation}
2240	  identifies the stalk \((F \triangleleft g)_x\) of the whiskered morphism with the
2241	  map
2242	  \[
2243	    \mathrm{id}_{F_x} \otimes g_x \;=\; \mathtt{LinearMap.lTensor}\,F_x\,g_x
2244	      \;\colon\; F_x \otimes_{R_x} M_x \longrightarrow F_x \otimes_{R_x} N_x .
2245	  \]
2246	
2247	  \emph{(c) Conclusion.} Tensoring the \(R_x\)-linear equivalence
2248	  \(g_x : M_x \xrightarrow{\sim} N_x\) of (a) on the left by the identity of \(F_x\)
2249	  yields an \(R_x\)-linear equivalence
2250	  \(\mathtt{LinearMap.lTensor}\,F_x\,g_x = \mathrm{id}_{F_x} \otimes g_x\)
2251	  (this is \(\mathtt{LinearEquiv.lTensor}\); it needs \emph{no} flatness of \(F_x\),
2252	  because lifting an isomorphism through \(F_x \otimes_{R_x} (-)\) only uses the
2253	  functoriality of the tensor product, and the inverse is supplied by the inverse
2254	  equivalence). By (b), \((F \triangleleft g)_x\) is therefore an isomorphism of
2255	  \(\mathtt{Ab}\)-stalks for every \(x\). Applying the converse half of the d.1-bridge
2256	  \cref{lem:W_implies_stalkwise_iso} to the morphism \(F \triangleleft g\) gives
2257	  \(F \triangleleft g \in J.W\), and in particular it is \(J\)-locally injective
2258	  (\(\mathtt{GrothendieckTopology.W.isLocallyInjective}\)). No flatness, no local
2259	  triviality, and the whiskering object \(F\) is arbitrary.
2260	
2261	  The statement holds over any Grothendieck site \(J\) satisfying
2262	  \(J.\mathtt{WEqualsLocallyBijective}\,\mathtt{Ab}\), and is applied in particular
2263	  at the topological site \(\mathtt{Opens}\,X\) of a scheme, where the stalk
2264	  arguments of (a)--(c) are available and through which the unconditional associator
2265	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso} is realized.
2266	\end{proof}

2268	\section{\(\otimes\)-invertibility and the commutative-monoid group-law engine}
2269	\label{sec:tensorobj_invertibility}
2270	
2271	The relative Picard group law is assembled from \emph{\(\otimes\)-invertibility}
2272	and the monoidal coherence isomorphisms of
2273	\cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso,lem:tensorobj_unit_iso,lem:tensorobj_comm_iso},
2274	following Mathlib's Picard-group idiom (\(\mathtt{Module.Invertible}\),
2275	\(\mathtt{CommRing.Pic} = \mathrm{Units}(\mathrm{Skeleton}(\mathtt{ModuleCat}\,R))\),
2276	\(\mathtt{instCommGroupPic}\); \texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.PicardGroup}) as the
2277	design template. Under the route~(e) fallback the inverse of an invertible object
2278	is carried by the predicate itself, so neither the open-immersion
2279	restriction-compatibility isomorphism \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso} nor a
2280	separate tensor-inverse lemma is needed there. Under the PRIMARY locally-trivial
2281	route, by contrast, both \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso} (via the PRIMARY proof
2282	of \cref{lem:islocallyinjective_whisker_of_W}) and the tensor-inverse lemma
2283	\cref{lem:tensorobj_inverse_invertible} (supplying inverses to
2284	\cref{lem:tensorobj_isoclass_commgroup}) are on the critical path.

1836	\section{The varying-ring stalk--tensor commutation (d.2) and the unconditional
1837	left-whiskering}
1838	\label{sec:tensorobj_stalk_tensor}
1839	
1840	This section supplies the one genuinely Mathlib-absent ingredient on which the
1841	\emph{unconditional} associator \cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso} rests: the commutation
1842	of the stalk functor with the relative-ring tensor product of presheaves of modules
1843	(``d.2''). Together with the already-built \(R_x\)-linear stalk comparison
1844	(\cref{lem:stalk_linear_map}, ``d.1'') it closes the single open left-whiskering
1845	obligation for an \emph{arbitrary} whiskering object --- with no flatness and no
1846	local-triviality hypothesis --- which is exactly what makes the sheafification
1847	transport of the presheaf associator work for all modules. The construction is to be
1848	formalized in the dedicated Lean file
1849	\(\mathtt{AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/TensorObjSubstrate/StalkTensor.lean}\).
1850	
1851	\begin{lemma}
1852	[Stalk of a tensor product (d.2): varying-ring stalk--tensor commutation]
1853	  \label{lem:stalk_tensor_commutation}
1854	  \lean{PresheafOfModules.stalkTensorIso}
1855	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj, lem:stalk_linear_map}
1856	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Tag (Modules on Ringed Spaces, \S Tensor product),
1857	  % Lemma lemma-stalk-tensor-product (stalk of a tensor product of modules is the
1858	  % tensor product of the stalks over the stalk ring)
1859	  % (read from references/stacks-modules.tex, L2332-L2344).
1860	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
1861	  % "\begin{lemma}
1862	  % \label{lemma-stalk-tensor-product}
1863	  % Let $(X, \mathcal{O}_X)$ be a ringed space.
1864	  % Let $\mathcal{F}$, $\mathcal{G}$ be $\mathcal{O}_X$-modules.
1865	  % Let $x \in X$. There is a canonical isomorphism
1866	  % of $\mathcal{O}_{X, x}$-modules
1867	  % $$
1868	  % (\mathcal{F} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{G})_x
1869	  % =
1870	  % \mathcal{F}_x \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_{X, x}} \mathcal{G}_x
1871	  % $$
1872	  % functorial in $\mathcal{F}$ and $\mathcal{G}$.
1873	  % \end{lemma}"
1874	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], Modules on Ringed Spaces,
1875	  Lemma~\texttt{lemma-stalk-tensor-product}.}
1876	  Let \(R : (\mathtt{Opens}\,X)^{op} \to \mathtt{CommRingCat}\) be the structure
1877	  presheaf of a scheme \(X\), and let \(A, B\) be presheaves of
1878	  \(R\)-modules (objects of \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\,(R \circ
1879	  \mathtt{forget_2}\,\mathtt{CommRingCat}\,\mathtt{RingCat})\)). For each point
1880	  \(x \in X\) there is a canonical isomorphism of \((R \circ
1881	  \mathtt{forget_2}).\mathtt{stalk}\,x\)-modules
1882	  \[
1883	    (A \otimes^{p} B).\mathtt{stalk}\,x
1884	      \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\;
1885	    A.\mathtt{stalk}\,x \;\otimes_{R.\mathtt{stalk}\,x}\; B.\mathtt{stalk}\,x,
1886	  \]
1887	  natural in \(A\) and \(B\), where \(\otimes^{p}\) is the presheaf-of-modules tensor
1888	  product \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.Monoidal.tensorObj}\).
1889	\end{lemma}
1890	
1891	\begin{proof}
1892	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj, lem:stalk_linear_map}
1893	  \leanok
1894	  The stalk at \(x\) is the filtered colimit over the neighbourhoods \(U \ni x\) of
1895	  the sections functor, and sectionwise the tensor product is computed pointwise:
1896	  \((A \otimes^{p} B)(U) = A(U) \otimes_{R(U)} B(U)\)
1897	  (\(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.Monoidal.tensorObj\_obj}\)). The neighbourhood system
1898	  \(\{U \ni x\}\) is cofiltered under inclusion, so the colimit is filtered. The
1899	  isomorphism is assembled in five named stages. Stages (i)--(ii) construct the
1900	  forward additive comparison map and its germ characterisation; stages (iii)--(v)
1901	  upgrade it to an \(R_x\)-linear map, construct the reverse map, and check mutual
1902	  inversion.
1903	
1904	  \emph{(i) Per-neighbourhood balanced bilinear map and its section-level descent}
1905	  (\(\mathtt{stalkTensorBilin}\), \(\mathtt{stalkTensorDescU}\)). For a fixed
1906	  neighbourhood \(U \ni x\) the assignment
1907	  \[
1908	    (a, b) \;\longmapsto\; \mathrm{germ}_x\,a \,\otimes\, \mathrm{germ}_x\,b,
1909	    \qquad a \in A(U),\ b \in B(U),
1910	  \]
1911	  is \(R(U)\)-balanced bilinear into \(A_x \otimes_{R_x} B_x\): it is additive in each
1912	  variable, and for \(r \in R(U)\) the two ways of moving \(r\) across the tensor agree
1913	  after passing to germs, since \(\mathrm{germ}_x(r \cdot a) = (\mathrm{germ}_x r)
1914	  \cdot \mathrm{germ}_x a\) and the stalk tensor is balanced over
1915	  \(R_x = R.\mathtt{stalk}\,x\) (the germ--scalar compatibility of
1916	  \cref{lem:stalk_linear_map}). By the universal property of the relative tensor
1917	  product it descends to an additive map on the section module
1918	  \[
1919	    \mathtt{stalkTensorDescU}_U \;\colon\;
1920	      (A \otimes^{p} B)(U) = A(U) \otimes_{R(U)} B(U)
1921	        \;\longrightarrow\; A_x \otimes_{R_x} B_x,
1922	    \qquad a \otimes b \longmapsto \mathrm{germ}_x a \otimes \mathrm{germ}_x b.
1923	  \]
1924	
1925	  \emph{(ii) Colimit descent to the forward comparison map}
1926	  (\(\mathtt{stalkTensorDesc}\), \(\mathtt{stalkTensorDesc\_germ\_tmul}\)). The
1927	  maps \(\mathtt{stalkTensorDescU}_U\) are compatible with the restriction maps of the
1928	  presheaf \(A \otimes^{p} B\) as \(U\) shrinks toward \(x\) (a smaller neighbourhood
1929	  computes the same germs), so they form a cocone over the filtered diagram
1930	  \(\{(A \otimes^{p} B)(U)\}_{U \ni x}\). The induced map out of the colimit is the
1931	  forward additive comparison map
1932	  \[
1933	    \mathtt{stalkTensorDesc} \;\colon\;
1934	      (A \otimes^{p} B).\mathtt{stalk}\,x \;\longrightarrow\; A_x \otimes_{R_x} B_x,
1935	  \]
1936	  characterised on germs of simple tensors by
1937	  \(\mathtt{stalkTensorDesc}\bigl(\mathrm{germ}_x(a \otimes b)\bigr) =
1938	  \mathrm{germ}_x a \otimes \mathrm{germ}_x b\)
1939	  (\(\mathtt{stalkTensorDesc\_germ\_tmul}\)). This is the forward half of the asserted
1940	  isomorphism (\cref{lem:stalk_tensor_desc_forward}).
1941	
1942	  \emph{(iii) \(R_x\)-linearity packaging} (\(\mathtt{stalkTensorLinearMap}\)). The
1943	  additive map of (ii) is upgraded to an \(R.\mathtt{stalk}\,x\)-linear map: on a germ
1944	  \(\mathrm{germ}_x r\) of a section \(r \in R(U)\) one checks
1945	  \(\mathtt{stalkTensorDesc}\bigl((\mathrm{germ}_x r)\cdot \xi\bigr) =
1946	  (\mathrm{germ}_x r)\cdot \mathtt{stalkTensorDesc}(\xi)\) on germs of simple tensors
1947	  and extends by additivity. The one subtlety is a \emph{carrier-duality obstacle}:
1948	  the section-level tensor \(A(U) \otimes_{R(U)} B(U)\) is a module over the
1949	  \(\mathtt{RingCat}\) carrier \((R \circ \mathtt{forget_2})(U)\), whereas the natural
1950	  scalar \(r : R(U)\) lives in the \(\mathtt{CommRingCat}\) carrier \(R(U)\); the two
1951	  carriers are canonically identified but not definitionally equal, so the
1952	  scalar-pulling lemma \(\mathtt{TensorProduct.smul\_tmul'}\) applies only after
1953	  inserting a canonical \(\mathtt{RingEquiv}\) bridge between the
1954	  \(\mathtt{CommRingCat}\) and \(\mathtt{RingCat}\) carriers. Once that bridge is
1955	  in place the linearity computation mirrors the germ-representative pattern used
1956	  for the d.1 stalk map \(\mathtt{stalkLinearMap}\) (\cref{lem:stalk_linear_map}).
1957	
1958	  \emph{(iv) The reverse map.} The map
1959	  \[
1960	    A_x \otimes_{R_x} B_x \;\longrightarrow\; (A \otimes^{p} B).\mathtt{stalk}\,x
1961	  \]
1962	  is built by the universal property of the tensor product over \(R_x\) from the
1963	  \(R_x\)-bilinear map of the two stalks
1964	  \[
1965	    (\mathrm{germ}_x a,\ \mathrm{germ}_x b) \;\longmapsto\;
1966	      \mathrm{germ}_x\bigl(a|_{U \cap V} \otimes b|_{U \cap V}\bigr),
1967	  \]
1968	  where \(a \in A(U)\) and \(b \in B(V)\) are chosen representatives; this is a
1969	  \emph{nested} colimit descent, descending first the \(A\)-variable out of the
1970	  filtered colimit \(\{A(U)\}_{U \ni x}\) and then the \(B\)-variable out of
1971	  \(\{B(V)\}_{V \ni x}\), passing to the common refinement \(U \cap V\) to form the
1972	  simple tensor and its germ. Well-definedness on germs (independence of the chosen
1973	  representatives and of the neighbourhoods \(U, V\)) is exactly filteredness of the
1974	  neighbourhood system.
1975	
1976	  It remains to check that the reverse map is \(R_x\)-bilinear, and in particular that
1977	  its underlying bilinear map is \emph{balanced} over \(R_x\), i.e. that pulling a scalar
1978	  out of the left variable agrees with pulling it out of the right. The key point is to
1979	  establish this balancing \emph{at the stalk level}, with every scalar living in the
1980	  stalk ring \(R_x = R.\mathtt{stalk}\,x\) throughout. Concretely, for a germ
1981	  \(\mathrm{germ}_x r\) of a section \(r \in R(W)\) one verifies the identity
1982	  \[
1983	    \mathtt{revBihom}\bigl((\mathrm{germ}_x r)\cdot \mathrm{germ}_x a,\ \mathrm{germ}_x b\bigr)
1984	      \;=\; (\mathrm{germ}_x r)\cdot \mathtt{revBihom}\bigl(\mathrm{germ}_x a,\ \mathrm{germ}_x b\bigr),
1985	  \]
1986	  and symmetrically on the right variable, where the scalar \(\mathrm{germ}_x r \in R_x\)
1987	  never leaves the stalk ring. This holds because of the germ--scalar compatibility
1988	  \(\mathrm{germ}_x(r \cdot s) = (\mathrm{germ}_x r)\cdot(\mathrm{germ}_x s)\) of the germ
1989	  map \(R(W) \to R_x\) (the Lean lemma \(\mathtt{germ\_smul}\)) — the same germ--scalar
1990	  compatibility already used in stages (i) and (iii). Multiplying a representative
1991	  \(r \cdot a\) and then taking germs produces \((\mathrm{germ}_x r)\cdot \mathrm{germ}_x a\),
1992	  so the action of \(r\) is transparently the \(R_x\)-action on the stalk and may be
1993	  moved freely across the tensor of stalks, where balancedness over \(R_x\) is built into
1994	  \(A_x \otimes_{R_x} B_x\). Because the whole computation takes place over the single
1995	  ring \(R_x\), no carrier identification intervenes and the balancing follows directly.
1996	
1997	  One must \emph{not} instead reduce this balancing to a section-level identity. If the
1998	  scalar were pushed through the presheaf restriction to a common neighbourhood \(W\) and
1999	  the balancing read off inside the section module \(A(W) \otimes_{R(W)} B(W)\), then the
2000	  scalar action appearing there is the one produced by the presheaf-of-modules structure
2001	  of \(A\) via restriction of scalars: it lives over the \(\mathtt{RingCat}\) carrier
2002	  \((R \circ \mathtt{forget_2})(W)\), \emph{not} over the \(\mathtt{CommRingCat}\) carrier
2003	  \(R(W)\) that annotates the section tensor. This is precisely the
2004	  \(\mathtt{CommRingCat}\)-vs-\(\mathtt{RingCat}\) carrier-duality obstacle already met in
2005	  stage (iii), here compounded by the extra restriction-of-scalars wrapper, so the naive
2006	  ``move the scalar across the tensor'' step (\(\mathtt{TensorProduct.smul\_tmul'}\)) does
2007	  not apply at the section level without first inserting the carrier bridge. Working at the
2008	  stalk level as above sidesteps this wrapper entirely.
2009	
2010	  Where a \(\mathtt{CommRingCat}\)-vs-\(\mathtt{RingCat}\) carrier identification is
2011	  genuinely unavoidable, it is the \emph{same} canonical bridge already invoked for the
2012	  stage-(iii) \(R_x\)-linearity packaging (the \(\mathtt{stalkTensorDescU\_smul}\)/%
2013	  \(\mathtt{stalkTensorLinearMap}\) step, \cref{lem:stalk_tensor_linear_map}): the two
2014	  carriers are canonically identified but not definitionally equal, and the same
2015	  \(\mathtt{RingEquiv}\) bridge resolves the obstruction here. Thus the stage-(iv)
2016	  balancing reuses the technique already resolved in stage (iii) rather than re-deriving
2017	  it.
2018	
2019	  \emph{(v) Mutual inversion on germ generators} (\(\mathtt{stalkTensorIso}\)). The two
2020	  composites are checked to be identities on generators. The composite
2021	  \(A_x \otimes_{R_x} B_x \to (A \otimes^{p} B).\mathtt{stalk}\,x \to A_x
2022	  \otimes_{R_x} B_x\) is the identity on a simple tensor \(\mathrm{germ}_x a \otimes
2023	  \mathrm{germ}_x b\) by the germ characterisation \(\mathtt{stalkTensorDesc\_germ\_tmul}\)
2024	  of (ii), and extends to all of \(A_x \otimes_{R_x} B_x\) by
2025	  \(\mathtt{TensorProduct.induction\_on}\). The opposite composite
2026	  \((A \otimes^{p} B).\mathtt{stalk}\,x \to A_x \otimes_{R_x} B_x \to (A \otimes^{p}
2027	  B).\mathtt{stalk}\,x\) is the identity on a germ \(\mathrm{germ}_x(a \otimes b)\) of
2028	  a simple tensor by the same characterisation; since germs generate the stalk and the
2029	  germ maps are jointly epimorphic (\(\mathtt{stalk\_hom\_ext}\)), together with
2030	  \(\mathtt{TensorProduct.induction\_on}\) applied to each section, the composite is the
2031	  identity. Bundling the \(R_x\)-linear forward map of (iii) with the reverse map of
2032	  (iv) and these two inversion identities yields the asserted isomorphism
2033	  \(\mathtt{stalkTensorIso}\).
2034	
2035	  Naturality in \(A, B\) is inherited from naturality of the sectionwise tensor and of
2036	  the germ maps. This is the presheaf-of-modules, varying-ring analogue of the Stacks
2037	  lemma above; the only subtlety beyond the ringed-space statement is that the ground
2038	  ring varies with \(U\), handled by the ``tensor of filtered colimits over the colimit
2039	  ring'' identification together with the carrier-duality bridge of (iii). Conceptually
2040	  the tensor product of modules commutes with filtered colimits in each variable and
2041	  the ground ring \(R(U)\) itself colimits to the stalk ring \(R.\mathtt{stalk}\,x\),
2042	  which is the reason the forward map (ii) and the reverse map (iv) are mutually
2043	  inverse.
2044	\end{proof}
